ekanant

body:
> I'm doubt about the logic in unique function why is has to check `h.key.indexOf('$') > 0`

That's from #9315 and it's necessary because React "escapes" keys by prepending `.$` while remapping `children` into an array.
Relevant lines of code ([facebook/react@a724a3b5/.../ReactChildren.js][ReactChildren@a724a3b5]):

```js
const SEPARATOR = '.'; // line 22
...

function escape(key: string) {
  ...
  return '$' + escapedString; // line 41
}

function getElementKey(element: any, index: number) {
    ...
    return escape('' + element.key); // line 71
    ...
}

function mapIntoArray() {
    ...
    const childKey =
      nameSoFar === '' ? SEPARATOR + getElementKey(child, 0) : nameSoFar; // line 116
    ...
}
```

In Preact, `children` is already an array; no remapping necessary. So I'm assuming it's just dumped onto DOM without changing values. Quote from https://preactjs.com/guide/v10/differences-to-react#children-api:
> For Preact this is generally unnecessary, and we recommend using the built-in array methods instead. In Preact, `props.children` is either a Virtual DOM node, an empty value like `null`, or an Array of Virtual DOM nodes. The first two cases are the simplest and most common, since it's possible to use or return `children as-is

I see you've created https://github.com/preactjs/next-plugin-preact/issues/51, but I'm not sure if that's the right repository. Perhaps preact/compat is where such adjustments need to be made, since that's the Preact->React compat layer. Anyway, for now I've manually put `.$` before all my keys (similar to what you did with `my_key$`), and NextJS' dedupe logic works.

[ReactChildren@a724a3b5]: https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/a724a3b578dce77d427bef313102a4d0e978d9b4/packages/react/src/ReactChildren.js

author: simontong

body:
I don't believe it is an actual bug, but more of a feature request, since you are describing some functionality that just does not exist as of today. (Unless we document it otherwise. Can you confirm?).

From the source code, it looks like we only look for changes in the `pages` directory in dev mode: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/a5bfc1eecc34e176f9f27955fde7b16e17de3c5e/packages/next/server/dev/next-dev-server.ts#L254

If you actually mean adding files when running in production (eg `next start`), that's expected behavior. See the docs:

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving

>  Only assets that are in the public directory at build time will be served by Next.js. Files added at runtime won't be available.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Make sure you are using Webpack 5 by upgrading to a newer version of Next.js. 

It introduced deterministic hashes whenever the content has not changed.

https://nextjs.org/blog/next-9-5#webpack-5-support-beta

You could also, move the build step out and share the build artifacts with the different instances, instead of building in them individually.
author: balazsorban44

body: I had the same issue, and came to the same conclusion; Use an EFS filesystem for sharing artifacts and handling incremental updates (e.g. next image caches and so on).

body:
Try removing pages/configuration incrementally, until you are left with some code that you can share in a repository or on something like https://codesandbox.io or https://stackblitz.com and it still reproduces the issue. This way, you might even be able to find the source of the issue, realizing that it's not a problem with Next.js but something in your own code.

If the issue can be reproduced with the `canary` version of Next.js, please come back with a link to your code, and we will have a closer look! :green_heart:

From the error [here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/32980#issuecomment-1004826488) though, it looks like there is a problem with your `.babelrc` file and how it is saved regarding the end of the line. :thinking:

body:
The problem originates from the `resolve`-package. Simply pin it to `v1.20.0` to solve the error until a fix is released.

```
"resolutions": {
    "resolve": "1.20.0"
  }
```

Related bug there: https://github.com/browserify/resolve/issues/264

author: Vanals

body:
Using this very simple code:

```js
// pages/_app.jx
import Head from "next/head"

export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <>
      <Head>
        <title key="title">TITLE</title>
        <meta key="description" name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
      </Head>
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </>
  )
}
```

And running `next build && next export` produces an HTML containing the correct elements in `head`:

```js
// out/index.html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

<head>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width" />
  <meta charSet="utf-8" />
  <title>TITLE</title>
  <meta name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
// ...
```

The problem seems to be specific for your use case and better asked in the [discussions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions) section.

author: balazsorban44

body:
Thank you for raising this issue. This is actually expected when you have a `rewrites` in your config and is vaguely hinted at in the docs here: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization

> During prerendering, the router's query object will be empty since we do not have query information to provide during this phase. After hydration, Next.js will trigger an update to your application to provide the route parameters in the query object.

So what is happening in your code is that the page gets statically optimized at build time without query params.

Rewrites can have params that we need to provide in the query e.g. `/some/:path` so if any `rewrites` are present, we trigger a re-render iterating over the rewrites to provide the parsed params.

We will try to make this more clear in the docs.
author: balazsorban44

body:
> Rewrites can have params that we need to provide in the query e.g. /some/:path so if any rewrites are present, we trigger a re-render iterating over the rewrites to provide the parsed params.

Mind providing more implementation details around this please? Specifically, I'm confused by query params that are static and not dynamic.

```
{
    source: 'home/:path/',
    destination: 'home?dynamic-query=:path',
},
{
    source: 'home/:path/',
    destination: 'home/:path/?static-query=static',
},
``` 

Since `static-query=static` is already present in the destination, can't that be passed as part of the router query? 

> So what is happening in your code is that the page gets statically optimized at build time without query params.

What about pages that are ISR? Since it's runtime, what are the limitations to access the query params for ISR pages?
author: PsyGik

body:
I understand the reason for this to happen, but is there a userland solution? 
Especially when using `Date`s via hydration, this kills referential equality and causes problems all over the place.
If I never use query params, is there a way to opt-out of this behavior?

[@ijjk said](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30720#issuecomment-956397094) that `isReady` could be used:

> As mentioned in the issue `isReady` can be used to defer logic that relies on the query information.

..but in my case, the double render problems have nothing to do with route-specific code or a global store/entrypoint. It's just that a lot of my code relies on hydrated data, so in reality I shouldn't need to check for `isReady` in every single `useEffect` where hydrated data is used.

---

This is a reocurring issue and I would highly appreciate if this is explained in the docs in more detail:
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/30720
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/27717
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/27224

The explanation right now does not make it clear that a double render will be the result of using `rewrites`.


---

_Context:_

In my case, I render huge charts, so a double initial render is causing massive problems. Specifically, I use [hydration with React Query](https://react-query.tanstack.com/guides/ssr#using-hydration) and `Date` objects will therefore trigger `useEffect`s all over the place, because of missing referential equality in the dependency arrays. I could e.g. use ISO strings and work around this problem, but in my opinion, this should not be a problem in the first place.
author: bennettdams

body: @bennettdams not directly related to this issue, but have you considered memoizing your components?
author: vtenfys

body:
> @bennettdams not directly related to this issue, but have you considered memoizing your components?

@valerie-makes My answer is also off-topic now: Sure, I already do. I have a lot of performance optimizations in place, e.g. also `useMemo` where needed, but **no** memoization (even `React.memo`) will help you when the referential equality of variables is not given, as it is the case here with the double hydration. 

That's what I meant with...
> I could e.g. use ISO strings and work around this problem, but in my opinion, this should not be a problem in the first place.

body:
> I had the same issue, 404 pages only when deploying on Vercel and the problem was that i was not passing the basePath to the `rewrite` function, the solution was to use something like this
> 
> 
> 
> ```ts
> 
> import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'
> 
> 
> 
> export default function middleware(req: NextRequest) {
> 
>     const { pathname, basePath } = req.nextUrl
> 
> 
> 
>     const hostname = req.headers.get('host')
> 
> 
> 
>     // does not work
> 
>     // `/_hosts/${hostname}${pathname}`
> 
> 
> 
>     return NextResponse.rewrite(
> 
>         new URL(
> 
>             `${basePath}/_hosts/${hostname}${pathname}`,
> 
>             req.nextUrl.origin,
> 
>         ).toString(),
> 
>     )
> 
> }
> 
> 
> 
> ```
> 
> 
> 
> The same problem probably exists when using i18n, you need to pass the base paths manually
> 
> 
> 
> PS: i made a full repo with an example of this working [here](https://github.com/remorses/repro-nextjs-middleware-rewrite-ssg-bug)

This didn't work for me unfortunately. Still getting a 404 using the approach above.

```

   new URL(
      `${basePath}/${locale}/`,
      origin
   ).toString()
);

author: jeremylvln

body:
@IamBlueSlime I'm still struggling with it, because it seems that my workaround was not really working.

### I was doing the following:
- Generate a JSON file right before "next build" and putting it into `node_modules/my-generated/config.json`
- Importing in the middleware via `import Config from 'my-generated/config.json'`

### The problem:
I needed to create another dummy JSON to run the "next build" successfully. Since middleware code runs only on backend side and I was assuming that I could update the placed JSON file on Server-Startup. But actually the `node_modules` packages are pre-bundled, too. So I had no influence.

### Current "solution"
Before running `next start` I replace the content of the `.next/server/pages/_middleware.js` inside the build directory with the environment variable from my runtime using regex. This has of course the disadvantage that my "hack" can break once NextJS changes the critical parts in future versions.

It is pretty sad, because I cannot imagine that it would be hard to support the Runtime-Env for the NextJS team inside middlewares. 

But maybe @balazsorban44 can explain the reasons why they don't want to support it.

author: anthonysidesap

body:
Due to the Serverless nature of Vercel, it needs to be able to statically analyze all the [files that it needs to include](https://github.com/vercel/nft).

To make it work, simply make sure that you read files using `process.cwd()` together with `path.join`

This will work, see the [deployed example](https://33133.vercel.app/):

```js
  const theFile = await readFile(
    join(process.cwd(), "./public/the-file.json"),
    "utf-8"
  ).catch(() => console.log('Failed to load "the-file.json"'))

  const theOtherFile = await readFile(
    join(process.cwd(), "./not-public/the-other-file.json"),
    "utf-8"
  ).catch(() => console.log('Failed to load "the-other-file.json"'))
```

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching#reading-files-use-processcwd

Also note, that you don't have to import `path` and `fs` inside `getServerSideProps`. Simply import them at the top of the file. Next.js eliminates those from the client-side bundle.

> Note: You can import modules in top-level scope for use in getServerSideProps. Imports used in getServerSideProps will not be bundled for the client-side.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching#getserversideprops-server-side-rendering

body:
So this is actually intentional.

In your use case, sending large files from a single Next.js instance isn't performant as the user could be far away or the connection gets broken so it's better to use a CDN which is designed for handling large assets.

The error document points this out as well:

> If you need to support sending large files to the client, you should consider using a dedicated media host for those assets.

https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/api-routes-body-size-limit
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44, Prior to opening this issue I read through all the relevant documentation. I'm aware that it is intentional.

However, in our use case, these are private files (requires authentication check), a lesser-used feature in larger application, and all users are in the same country. Therefore we could not justify a **CDN**. Even a **dedicated media host** would be overkill. As we grow this may change but not any time soon.

We could certainly start a **custom server** but that seems like a workaround for _red tape_. Plus we'd lose the `npm run dev` **hot reloading** advantage for those particular endpoints.

On the other hand, it would be so easy to add a config switch, and many developers are in use cases like ours, where, nobody benefits from overengineering. That is, **until they can grow** their company to a larger scale.

So, I'm just thinking through the Next.js team's motivation for adding this restriction. What your team has done makes sense in certain use cases, but not for everyone. I'm particularly concerned about the **Error** coming in a future version.

My team would be glad to create a PR for a config switch. What do you think?

author: 700software

body:
@balazsorban44 Please consider reopening the issue or participating in the conversation here.

I'm also afraid of the time where this will produce an error.

Not everyone is using Vercel / Cloud services, there is a world beyond this : i'm self-hosting on a dedicated server, thus i'm fully responsible for the resource usage on my server. There can be a warning to tell me, I have no issue with that. But I would like to be fully responsible for my actions and thus : 
 * Be able to disable that warning if I judge it doesn't apply
 * Still be able to use that function if it fits my use case

Especially since it's an easy solution to implement without going all out for a CDN which is probably overkill in many, many situations. It adds an extra layer of complexity and cost for little to no gain in many use cases.

My personal use case is gating the downloads behind an authentication mechanism ( next-auth ).

To give you some numbers : 
 * The gated files are about 30MB and are downloaded 20 000 times per day in my case (which amounts to about 600GB per day)
 * I don't have any performance or CPU usage issues (quite the contrary, it's really low, congrats !). Same with network. It's just working like a charm.
 * I have NO complains about users having slow downloads or issue with this mechanism
 * Website is working like a charm

I agree with @700software here : give us a way to disable this warning, and forbid or do whatever you want on Vercel to prevent it for being used. Restricting it for everyone is just too broad.

I personally think you're implementing a Vercel/Cloud oriented warning inside Next.js, which is not the place for that limit to be.
author: Tetedeiench

body:
@balazsorban44 Hoping you can reopen this issue and allow a PR to be made. I do not use Vercel and have a use case in streaming large files back to the client.

Trying to load large files first in memory or moving them from S3 and back again just won't work for me. 

We allow users to download their folders/files from S3 (kinda like Dropbox). When a folder download request comes in we pull from S3, create directories and zip the whole package up and stream it back to the client. For us to take that zip, push it back out to S3 and request a presigned URL is just not as performant (not to mention it can get costly) as streaming the data across.

I agree with both @Tetedeiench and @700software that there should be a way to configure this option.
author: crice88

body: Hi, I think a config to disable this warning similar to the [`externalResolver` page config](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/api-middlewares#custom-config) is reasonable, note this warning is relevant to deployments not leveraging serverless as well. If you are hosting in Docker and you are serving files from an API route this is much less optimal than serving directly from a CDN which handles disconnects/resuming serving the file from a location closest to the user.

However, because we are using SSR, this is not enough to prevent flickering, so we tried to use [antiflicker script provided by Google](https://developers.google.com/optimize#the_anti-flicker_snippet_code) but it sets opacity:0 to the whole page and this hurts web vitals (mainly LCP).

author: gabrielpioto

body:
Me and my team are struggling with this exact issue right now. I created the issue #33402 on documentation inconsistencies, but would also like to see some sort of a resolve to this.

Our use case is following: we use two `next/script` elements to load two external scripts: the first one loads a cookie consent SDK and the second loads our analytics library. Both of the script elements have their own `onLoad`s for initialization.

The problem is that the analytics library depends on the cookie consent SDK, so we need to load and initialize that one first. Also, both of these scripts need to be initialized before any React `useEffect` hooks are run, because that's where the tracking functions of the analytics library are to be used.

Now, because `onLoad`s for these scripts are just silently ignored, we can't do initialization on neither of these libraries.

My use case is to load the scripts to access the Google APIs (`https://accounts.google.com/gsi/client` and `https://apis.google.com/js/api.js`). body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

author: kepes

body:
Ah, I did not realize from your description you were referring to `next/head`, instead of the one in `_document`.

The documentation says this clearly:

> `title`, `meta` or any other elements (e.g. `script`) need to be contained as direct children of the Head element, or wrapped into maximum one level of `<React.Fragment>` or arrays—otherwise the tags won't be correctly picked up on client-side navigations.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/head

The easiest solution here is to just create your custom `Head` component with the `next/head` as the most outer component like so:

```js
// HeadMeta.js
import Head from "next/head"

export default function HeadMeta(props) {
  return (
    <Head>
      <title>{props.title}</title>
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Roboto:300,400,500,700&display=swap"
      />
    </Head>
  )
}
```

Note, that fonts should probably still be loaded in `_document`, not per page.

body: The Cache-Control header set by `next start` is correct, as it prevents accidental caching of responses that can't be cached by default. If the page uses SSR (getServerSideProps/getInitialProps) it will have the mentioned cache control header. If it's using SSG (getStaticProps or automatic static optimization) it will have `s-maxage=REVALIDATE_SECONDS, stale-while-revalidate` or if `revalidate` is not used `s-maxage=31536000, stale-while-revalidate`. If you want a different `Cache-Control` while using SSR you can use `res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'value_you_prefer')` and Next.js will keep it as-is in production. In development te `Cache-Control` header is overwritten to ensure every request is not cached.

body:
I also have this issue, works fine when running the dev server but fails when doing a production build. A wasm file looks to have moved to .next/server/static/wasm/... but it has a different hash?

Edit:
Just looking a bit deeper and the specified wasm file looks to be generated but is in `.next/server/chunks/static/wasm/[hash].wasm` not `.next/server/static/wasm/[hash].wasm` where it's looking for it. It looks as though Next prefixes the `webassemblyModuleFilename` defined in the webpack config with `chunks/` when creating the server build, but in the `webpack-runtime.js` file generated in the server folder it isn't prefixed?

I've also tried the with-webassembly example and get the same error when running a production build.

body:
Hi @balazsorban44 ,

I have created a sample repo using `npx create-next-app@latest` command to reproduce the bug. Please clone the code from https://github.com/harshal247software/nextjsworkerissue.git & run the `npm i` & then `npm run dev` 

It will give the error _error - ReferenceError: Worker is not defined_ 

I have taken the sample code given by [wasm-imagemagick](https://github.com/KnicKnic/WASM-ImageMagick#demos-and-examples). You can check their working example here https://codesandbox.io/s/wasm-imagemagick-basic-demo-y00u2. It is working fine with React but not with NextJS
author: harshal-ringe

body:
So there seem to be a few issues. Since `Worker` is not an [API in Node.js](https://nodejs.org/api/worker_threads.html#worker-threads), the SSR pass won't work in Next.js. You have to make sure that the code is only invoked in the browser.

From their docs, it seems that this is not something `wasm-imagemagick` wants to support anyway (only focused on browser), so what you could do is to dynamically import the package [in `doMagick`](https://github.com/harshal247software/nextjsworkerissue/blob/28024649f373549f2bab9946a1cf4d5d8d6c2d80/pages/index.js#L6) like so:

```js
const Magick = await import("wasm-imagemagick")
```

This way it only gets imported when being used (in the browser).

This brings up a different error though:

```js
SecurityError: Failed to construct 'Worker': Script at 'webpack-internal:///./node_modules/wasm-imagemagick/dist/src/magick.js' cannot be accessed from origin 'http://localhost:3000'.
```

Which I'm not entirely sure about, but in their README there is [this section](https://github.com/KnicKnic/WASM-ImageMagick#importing-the-library-in-your-project) with an **IMPORTANT** note to copy some files from `node_modules`. This sounds fragile and probably indicates some non-standard setup/hack.

I also found a more up-to-date package that seems to have the same purpose, you could test that one out: https://github.com/dlemstra/magick-wasm

For reference, `Worker` is supported in Next.js, when used correctly. See the following example: [`with-web-worker`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/with-web-worker)

So the issue is specific to `wasm-imagemagick`

body:
Hi, I could not reproduce this with your reproduction. 

Sounds like a rogue `react` package is interfering with your project.

This kind of issue can happen if you have used `yarn link` or `npm link`.

Does this reproduce on multiple machines, or only on yours? Since you mention "our pc's", could you please check this on other devices? That would indeed be helpful since we could narrow this down to either OS or user config level.)

You mention TypeScript, is this an indication that the issue does not reproduce when you don't set the `--typescript` flag? Can you reproduce the problem without it?

Others have suggested try upgrading, but you seemed to be frustrated that this is only available on `canary`. Today, we released a new `latest`, (`12.0.8` as of writing), so you might try again. If that does not help (considering this issue was reported to already use `12.0.8`), checking `canary` is still a good option, as it would indicate that the problem is indeed already solved, we only need to cut a new `latest` release.

The actual shown warning could be useful: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/invalid-project-dir-casing

I can also see there is a discussion already opened by you here: #32034

Since this is not a confirmed bug and there is no reliable reproduction, let's continue the discussion there, since it has already started.

I'll reach out to our team to see if anyone has a better idea about what is happening.

Further, I kindly ask you to refrain from negative/demanding phrases and have a read of our [Code of Conduct](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).

body:
I think this is the same issue that my brother and I ran into last night. 

We were trying to set up VSCode to use debugging and every time the debugger would stop, it would open the transpiled file in VSCode, instead of stopping in the typescript file.

The coverage reports were also inaccurate because they were reported on the transpiled file instead of the source. 

We finally bypassed the swc compiler for jest, (using ts-jest) and the result was lined up coverage files, and proper breakpoint debugging.

Something I noticed is that the transpiled files did not have a sourcemap url at the bottom of the files when VSCode would stop there, so it's possible that the swc compiler is missing some configuration.

FWIW this happens in the `example/with-jest` folder in this repository.

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

author: Downchuck

body:
This was related to multiple npm library folders... Figured out when I ran "npm run dev" vs "next dev" - the latter did not work, the former did. Two things were going very wrong: my npm installation went from /usr to /usr/local as a prefix; running the "next" command was using that /usr/bin/next, and things got quite confused between the project node modules and the global installation.

Going through all of this, I still think it may be related to the issue that @imconfused218 was hitting - it's just that the error was getting caught.

author: james-maher

body:
It seems this regression happened between 12.0.7 and 12.0.8. We have two Nextjs-projects with a working browserslist-extends on 12.0.7 like:
```
extends @myscope/browserslist-config-foo
```

Both break when upgrading to 12.0.8.

with errors  like :
```
error - ./node_modules/next/dist/client/dev/amp-dev.js
Error: Cannot find module '@myscope/browserslist-config-foo'
    at Array.reduce (<anonymous>)
```
author: DennisNeuendorfJimdo

body: The reproduction-repo form @ale-grosselle  shows the error (when you delete the package-lock.json). Pinning nextjs to 12.0.7. fixes the problem.
author: DennisNeuendorfJimdo

body: Running into the same issue, also a scoped browserslist config, any workarounds besides downgrading?
author: topaxi

body:
The bug seems to be introduced in this [commit](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/commit/3667eba38559e5ccb810bade4779cda3649f1413#diff-35e60cb9cfc6f14c2998799e7e5a28e5adfd6ededc273851531da3a1e506b0d4) by switching to a precompiled version of browserslist. 

I replaced the compiled import `require("next/dist/compiled/browserslist")`  to the former `require("browserslist")` which solved the issue.

body:
Because npm does not give us any way to mark "development" vs "production", so there's no differentiation between what should be needed in production vs development. 

If you want the Next.js app to run without node_modules and only the deps needed for the app you can use the new experimental flag `outputStandalone` that leverages the node-file-trace files to create a standalone Next.js app: https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing#automatically-copying-traced-files-experimental.

body:
Please note: this issue may be related to next.js jest compiler settings. See discussion here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/31152#discussioncomment-1951381

> We have found two issues related to the use of `next/jest` that causes problems with programs that rely on source maps.
> 
> 1. Source maps were not included, which ideally should be when running tests to get correct stack traces, etc.
> 2. The `jsc.transform.optimizer` setting would result in incorrect line/column mappings being returned in source maps 
> 
> Updating `getJestSWCOptions` to include `sourceMaps: true` and removing `jsc.transform.optimizer` settings fixes these problems:
> 
> https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/950eb0f48916e36e813e7aeb3d80cc24293fa0c2/packages/next/build/swc/options.js#L72

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

author: ivorpad

body:
@danr-za since I'm unable to reproduce this without using a custom server, the following reply applies to you as well as to @ivorpad.

When using middleware we always need to have an absolute URL to handle. If you run Next.js in server mode (`dev` or `start`) we figure out the port and host automatically when instantiating the server and then pass them as options for `NextServer`. While those options are not mandatory, they are required if you are using middleware so if you are running Next.js with a custom server (which seems to be the case for Netlify here), you must pass them.

body:
I am using a custom server, and I just upgraded from 12.0.0 to 12.0.8 and I am now getting this error. Before this, the middleware worked fine without any configuration. 

This is also not documented anywhere: https://nextjs.org/docs/middleware nor does it seems like there is any information to find on how to pass the custom server this information(except the port, which is already being passed).

This is very clearly an issue. At the very least, the fact that it isn't documented and it's a regression compared to 12.0.0 behavior makes this a valid issue, so why was this issue closed @balazsorban44 ?

EDIT:

This is kinda sorta documented here: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/cli#development

You can control it programmatically:

```ts
const app = next({ dev, customServer: true, hostname: "localhost", port });
```

body:
After some research, this seems to be intentional by Mongoose and not related to Next.js.

https://stackoverflow.com/a/19051909

The logic on this line:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/4d3b2ea426ee872532f987461d7fece16e67ad6a/examples/with-mongodb-mongoose/models/Pet.js#L59

prohibits recreating the model once added, which would throw the following error:

```js
OverwriteModelError: Cannot overwrite `Pet` model once compiled.
```

For reference, other ORMs (eg.: TypeORM) have options like `synchronize: true` for development, which will automatically update the models when there are changes.

body:
I found the issue. You tried to import types in .js files in multiple places.

Here is an example: https://github.com/PH4NTOMiki/nextjs-test-app-vercel-bug/blob/bfd44e7cc4e28dadcd9ea80c88cb38c9acb976f2/pages/index.js#L7

You have two options here:

1. convert the files to TypeScript (preferably still the [`type` import modifier](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-3-8.html#type-only-imports-and-export) to indicate to yourself that you are only importing a type). Next.js [supports TypeScript](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/typescript)
2. Use the JSDoc equivalent import. For the given example above, you could do `* @param {import("next").NextPageContext} ctx` instead. (You have done so in `lib/_fetch.js` which is the correct way.)

body:
I also want to point out that I had this exact same problem, where I assumed Next Middleware's `NextResponse.cookie` API used seconds for `maxAge` the same as every other cookie API. I was very confused when my cookie, which I expected to last an hour, only lasted for 3 seconds. 

I agree with the proposed expected behavior: Either document this deviation from the expected behavior, or introduce a breaking change to make `maxAge` be provided in seconds.
author: alexanderson1993

body: The same.
author: kodermax

body:
Seems like the cookie API derived from express which uses milliseconds as the unit https://expressjs.com/ja/api.html#res.cookie
It's also true that many other libraries use seconds as the unit. I think both makes sense.

If we want to change the behavior, maybe should do it by introducing a new API in conjunction with https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/31719 so that easier to migrate and less confusions for existing apps.

body:
I believe organizing files is a personal matter and hard to find a perfect structure. The few folders Next.js enforces are:

`public` for static assets: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving
`pages` for file-based routing: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/pages
`pages/api` for API routes: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-routes/introduction

Anything else is really up to the developer.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Hi, since you say it works locally, it sounds like an Amplify issue.

I checked their documentation at https://docs.amplify.aws/guides/hosting/nextjs/q/platform/js and it hasn't been updated for a while:

> Currently, Amplify doesn't fully support Image Component and Automatic Image Optimization available in Next.js 10. 

Next.js is now on version 12. ([Next.js 10](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-10) came out over a year ago)

From the above message, it can be assumed that Preview Mode might not be supported on Amplify. I suggest [raising this issue with them](https://github.com/aws-amplify/amplify-js), and if they verify it is a feature that is intended to work, please come back and we can have another look! :+1:

author: github-actions

body:
I wonder if [import assertions](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-assertions) can be useful here. Just like how you can't rely on file extensions for mime types, we can't rely on file extensions here (`.server.{js,ts,tsx}`). As you mentioned aliasing and all other dynamic importing shenanigans (`import(<expression>)` etc ) makes it impossible to _always_ cleverly figure out if a component is server, client or shard.  

```ts
import SomeComponentFromALibrary from '@library/component-name' assert { type: "server.js" };
```

Just an idea...
author: mohsen1

body: Yep I agree that import assertion is a good idea to hint the compiler. An alternative way is to create a no-op .server.js (or .client.js) file to re-export the lib.
author: shuding

body: After doing some investigation on import assertion we'd likely hold it so far and keep the convention of using `.server.js` and `.client.js` so far as it's recommended by react team. And solve shared components in other alternative way.

author: alc0034

body:
It turns out that our issue was related to an older version of next-i18next.
When pushing changes to the router, it would unset the locale of the i18next provider, causing the whole page to re-render, losing state.

After updating to the latest version, it keeps the locale around, and this issue went away.
author: Multiply

author: mfnash

body:
> It turns out that our issue was related to an older version of [next-i18next](https://linear.app/vercel/issue/NEXT-i18next). When pushing changes to the router, it would unset the locale of the i18next provider, causing the whole page to re-render, losing state.
> 
> After updating to the latest version, it keeps the locale around, and this issue went away.

@Multiply thanks for this. I was able to track that my whole `_app` was getting remounted, but I didn't think of `appWithTranslation` wrapper being the culprit. For anyone looking for more information, they fixed it in v10.3.0: https://github.com/i18next/next-i18next/releases/tag/v10.3.0 (I actually tested 10.2.0 vs 10.3.0 to see the difference.)

FROM node:16.13-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
# COPY --from=deps /app/.yarn  ./.yarn
COPY . .
RUN yarn install --immutable
RUN yarn build

# Production image, copy all the files and run next
FROM node:16.13-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app

ENV NODE_ENV production

RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs
RUN adduser -S nextjs -u 1001

# You only need to copy next.config.js if you are NOT using the default configuration
COPY --from=builder /app/next.config.js ./
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./package.json


# Automatically leverage output traces to reduce image size 
# https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/output-file-tracing
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

COPY --from=builder /app/.env.production.local ./

USER nextjs

EXPOSE 3000

ENV PORT 3000

# Next.js collects completely anonymous telemetry data about general usage.
# Learn more here: https://nextjs.org/telemetry
# Uncomment the following line in case you want to disable telemetry.
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED 1

CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

body:
If it helps, this is how I've handled it... it expects a long string which is tricky to format so something like the following makes that slightly more manageable and easier to track changes:

```js
{
        key: "Content-Security-Policy",
        value: [
            ["base-uri", ["'self'"]],
            ["default-src", ["'self'"]],
            ["connect-src", ["'self'", "example.com", "example.co.uk"]],
            ["font-src", ["'self'"]],
            ["form-action", ["'none'"]],
            ["frame-ancestors", ["'none'"]],
            ["img-src", ["'self'"]],
            ["object-src", ["'none'"]],
            ["script-src", ["'self'", "'unsafe-eval'"]],
            ["style-src", ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"]],
        ]
            .map(([key, value]) => `${key} ${value.join(" ")};`)
            .join(" "),
}
```

Note that `self` and `none` are keywords so are expected to be wrapped in quotes whereas to domains are not.

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body:
> You still **need** to provide the possible locales your app will support as explained higher up in the docs:

I think it is still not clear that the _locales_ AND the _defaultLocale_ are mandatory.    
Searching online for a way to get the HTTP header "Accept-Language" many suggests that you can simply use _useRouter_ and the provided _.locale_ property from it.    
It's not immediately clear that you are forced to choose a limited list of locales you want to support.

I don't want routing,  I need to just be able to provide a locale to my DatePicker, any possible locale (more precisely I have a list of 555 locales).  
Essentially I wanted only use the ability to detect the locale.  
After a week without beeing able to user useRouter I think the simple way, without using intrusive 3rd party packages, is to use a _middelware_ and parse the req.headers.get("accept-language") string myself.
Thank you.
author: alex-piccione

body:
Check out the `navigator.language` API if you only need the user's preferred language for your DatePicker:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Navigator/language

There is also an experimental `navigator.languages` which gives you a list so you can fall back if you don't support some of the languages in that list:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Navigator/languages

Ideally, all this could be part of the DatePicker component/library and you as the consumer should only need to set a single prop like `detectLanguage`.
author: balazsorban44

body:

That is was what I did initially (and what I always did when website where done just with vanilla JavaScript), 
but I cannot find a "place" (Page, getStaticProps, components) where _navigator_ or _window.navigator_ is not _undefined_.   
It's like it always executes "server side"...  
That's why I tried to use something _inside_ the framework.

It seems like I wasn't to far: https://github.com/vercel/examples/blob/main/edge-functions/i18n/pages/_middleware.ts
author: alex-piccione

body: Coming from something like Create React App I can understand the immediate reaction. It boils down to that Next.js will try to create a pre-rendered version of your page for a fast initial response, so you cannot access `window` inside components, you will have to find a safe place like `useEffect`, which only executes client-side. See https://nextjs.org/docs/migrating/from-create-react-app#safely-accessing-web-apis

body: I've digged a bit in the source code and I think this is because [getPreNextScripts](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fd1c56e66ab0d9612930efac6cb77985fb91fac7/packages/next/pages/_document.tsx#L75). Some attributes are forwarded but `defer` and `crossorigin` are overwritten, is this intentional?
author: albertodeago

body:
For `crossOrigin`, I have an answer for you:

https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/doc-crossorigin-deprecated

I'm not sure why this warning does not show up in development:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/fd1c56e66ab0d9612930efac6cb77985fb91fac7/packages/next/pages/_document.tsx#L828

Although it talks about `NextScript`, which is not the same as `next/script`, but comes from `next/document`. I'll double-check what is happening here.

I believe `defer` is intentional though, but I'll double-check that as well.

body:
Hi, thanks for reporting. I think I could reproduce some weird behavior. The issue I found was that when using `localhost` as an URL and there was no lock file generated yet, the dev server just hung, and the build failed with your description. Interestingly adding some other URLs to a different page and rendering that first in dev, the lock file is generated correctly. After that, visiting the page with the localhost import will work. ```js pages/confetti.js import confetti from "https://cdn.skypack.dev/canvas-confetti" import { useEffect } from "react" export default function Page() { useEffect(() => { confetti() }) return

Hello

} ``` Adding and visiting this page first created the lock file for me and after that the build passed also. We will need to look more into this.

author: github-actions

body:
This is expected.

https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization#how-it-works

> During prerendering, the router's query object will be empty since we do not have query information to provide during this phase. After hydration, Next.js will trigger an update to your application to provide the route parameters in the query object.

So query values are delayed for static pages. We don’t provide them immediately during hydration to prevent mismatches (since we cannot know about them when the page builds) so we trigger a second render with the updated query information.

body:
Hi @Anshuman71 

The issue is that the following is not statically analyzable:

```js
const jimpFont = path.resolve(jimp.FONT_SANS_32_BLACK);
```

The variable `jimp.FONT_SANS_32_BLACK` could be anything that jimp decides to [return at runtime](https://github.com/oliver-moran/jimp/blob/53ff9d1266207f7f674233f465ec358274510511/packages/plugin-print/src/index.js#L98).

If you change this line to a static string (cannot change at runtime), then Next.js will include the correct file at build time.

For example:

```ts
const plugin = require.resolve('@jimp/plugin-print');
const jimpFont = path.resolve(plugin, '../../fonts/open-sans/open-sans-32-black/open-sans-32-black.fnt');
```

Also is there really a need for getStaticPaths for the SSG? why can't we skip it and compile at build time props that will be added to all pages? body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body: If you use any of the server code in a component, it is going to be included in client bundles. You should calculate that state on server and pass it to the client via getServerSideProps or other data fetching functions. https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/overview

author: balazsorban44

body:
However, the document referrer is on client-side. I think it's  because when transition ocorrus a retrieve from new data json instead a document (html). So the browser can't update document-referrer info!
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Document/referrer @balazsorban44
author: PhilipeGatis

body:
Client-side navigation won't emit this change AFAIK, they are not "true" navigations. (In a sense that they won't make a new request to the server, but they will be announced for a11y purposes, etc.)

You can test this by going to your inspector on this page, write `window.history.pushState(null, null, "/some-page")` and call `document.referrer` and see that it did not update correctly.

Besides, there are probably other ways to achieve what you are trying to do. You could for example listen to a router event `routeChangeStart`:

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#usage-6

Hope that helps! :pray:

Hey @diberry! Sorry for the confusion. We cover this here: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props#write-server-side-code-directly 

Essentially what this means is that you shouldn't call an API Route from inside one of the getXXXXX functions. If your API route is making a call to an external CMS to fetch data, you can bring that calling logic into the getXXX function instead. 

We'll look to update the page you linked 🙏

body:
> Open about.tsx file in pages folder to see typescript error.

You are exporting the `<NewHero/>`  component as default, so you don't need the `.then` block when you are importing it.
```ts
const Component = dynamic(() => import("../components/Component").then(module => module.Component); // wrong
const Component = dynamic(() => import("../components/Component") // correct
```
Only use the .then module, when you are using named export from the file you are importing from.
```ts
// NewHero.tsx
function NewHero(){return ....}
export NewHero
.
.
// About.tsx
const Component = dynamic(() => import("../components/Component").then(module => module.NewHero);
```
for further reading: [NextJs Dynamic Import](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import)


> Open index.tsx file and corresponding page in browser, open Console you will see a warning: Did not expect server HTML to contain a `<section> in <div>`.

> Open furthermore.tsx file and corresponding page in browser, in Console you will see warning Text content did not match. Server: "Hero header" Client: "New Hero header"

These two warnings will be gone when you export the `<Hero/>` component in a normal way. 
```ts
export default Hero
```
I didn't find a solid reference for the usage of the `export default dynamic()` method, but personally, I am using it only with nextjs pages whenever I need this page to not render in the server-side 
```ts
export default dynamic(() => Promise.resolve(CartPage), { ssr: false });
```
This could be helpful if you are using the browser local storage to store the cart times, or when you are doing client-side authentication and need to prevent the app to render the UserProfile page, and so on..

But when talking about code splitting and lazy loading a component with nextjs, I always use dynamic import 

By the way, NextJs present a new API to handle client and server component based on `React18` but it is still on beta, you can read more from [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/react-18)

author: balazsorban44

body:
Yep, seeing the same issue.

I had actually run into this earlier on our project when I switched from `babel-jest` to `@swc-node/jest`.  I worked around this by using the `musl-libc` build of SWC instead, but that actually required doing a custom build of `libc.musl-x86_64.so.1` on a RHEL7 machine, committing it to our repo, and then setting `LD_LIBRARY_PATH="./path/to/musl-libc/"` in CI.

Not sure why that isn't working here.  I can see Next trying to load the `musl-libc` build, but failing to find that shared lib:

```
info  - Checking validity of types...
info  - Creating an optimized production build...

info  - Attempted to load @next/swc-linux-x64-gnu, but an error occurred: /lib64/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.18' not found (required by $OUR_PROJECT/node_modules/@next/swc-linux-x64-gnu/next-swc.linux-x64-gnu.node)

info  - Attempted to load @next/swc-linux-x64-gnux32, but it was not installed

info  - Attempted to load @next/swc-linux-x64-musl, but an error occurred: libc.musl-x86_64.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

I'll keep poking at this, but I'm wondering if maybe `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` isn't getting passed through to the process that is loading the binaries?

Overall, it looks like this would be fixed if Next could pull over those latest SWC build changes.  Can we get that applied soon?
author: markerikson

body:
It could be fixed with `--zig` cross toolchains with NAPI-RS v2: https://github.com/swc-project/swc/pull/3314

I'll fix it after https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34355 merge.

author: Yovach

body:
Here are two solutions that might help

## 1 - sw.js file with typesciprt capabilities

Create a sw.js file in your public directory and add the following to the top:

```ts
/// <reference lib="webworker" /> // 
// @ts-check
/** @type {ServiceWorkerGlobalScope} */
// @ts-ignore
const sw = self;
```

Explanation:

- Reference the webworker library to get the needed types for the service-worker
- Tell the typescript compiler to check this js file
- Define the type of the service worker using jsdoc
- Tell the typescript compiler to ignore the next line
- Reference the service worker through a new variable, that's because the "self" variable cannot have its type overridden which explains the ts-ignore comment

The drawbacks of this is that it's not quite a typescript file and so any explicit typings will need to be in the form of a jsdoc comment, so if you really want to have a typescript file here's solution 2:

## 2 - Manually handle a sw.ts file

Create a sw.ts file and add the following to the top:
```ts
/// <reference lib="webworker" />
const sw = self as unknown as ServiceWorkerGlobalScope; // we still need to override the "self" variable
```
Now run this command
```bash
npx tsc path/to/your/sw.ts --lib "WebWorker" --outDir public --watch
```
This will watch whenever your service worker changes and compile it again into the public directory, next will handle the rest
You can also use something like [concurrently](https://github.com/open-cli-tools/concurrently#readme) to run "next dev" with this command to watch everything at once

body: No. (But SSR appears to work without it, and it _does_ include "hello, world" in SSR output with `yarn dev` after first request.)
author: gaearon

body:
> after the first request

Without `concurrentFeatures` set, we use the legacy `renderToString` method, so we won't wait for Suspense. It seems like it's probably suspending the first time (hence the fallback) but not the second time because the module is already loaded.
author: devknoll

body: Ahhhh that's why it worked at all. I see.
author: gaearon

body: Yeah, this is one of several reasons that I think we'd really be in favor of an option to preserve React 17 Suspense SSR semantics unless explicitly opted in
author: devknoll

body: I see. So basically there's no way to try this now (though we always do SSG) because opting into the flag enables the runtime, etc. But then when that issue is fixed, we can try again.
author: gaearon

body:
> Yeah, this is one of several reasons that I think we'd really be in favor of an option to preserve React 17 Suspense SSR semantics unless explicitly opted in

You mean in renderToString? If the way you opt into the new "streaming" mode, even if buffered, is by upgrading to React 18, then you'd get both upgrades so there's no way to get into this intermediate state.

You can still get the problem with data being different but lots of legit use cases don't have those (React.lazy being one) issues. So I think you really need to discover it by seeing the bug in your library.
author: sebmarkbage

body: The way the client upgrade path works is that the existing API keeps working but warns you to rename to createRoot. So it's deprecated but you have an upgrade path. We could do the same for renderToString but it would be the same API signature so if you do need to keep it, it would be unnecessary. However, doing the exercise would force you to make a choice and hopefully use the streaming one. The end result would be that Next.js should only have the streaming one on though.
author: sebmarkbage

body: That makes sense. Let me see why this isn't already the case.
author: devknoll

body: Yeah, I think we can change this (I opened #33886). We should probably also make `next/dynamic` not require `concurrentFeatures` too.
