body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

author: github-actions

body:
Hi, a related issue has just been addressed by #33831, we should see in the next `canary` release it gets fixed.

Unfortunately given your reproduction, the error I am getting is different:

```sh
Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: require() of ES Module /.../node_modules/d3-array/src/index.js from /.../node_modules/reaviz/dist/index.cjs.js not supported.
Instead change the require of index.js in /.../node_modules/reaviz/dist/index.cjs.js to a dynamic import() which is available in all CommonJS modules.
```

This indicates a wrong usage of ESModules in the `reaviz` package. Looking at `d3-array`, it sets the `type: "module"` attribute and the `main` and `exports`. From their [v3 release](https://github.com/d3/d3-array/releases/tag/v3.0.0) it looks like they are now ESM-only.

Since `reaviz@11`, their intention seems to be the same, using ESM themselves: https://github.com/reaviz/reaviz/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#1100---91721

But it is not correctly set up. I suggest reaching out to the `reaviz` library authors about this, pointing them to this official Node.js documentation: https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#package-entry-points

TLDR; The package should not use the non-standard `module` field, but should prefer the `exports` field instead and should probably set the `type: "module"` property also.

You can mention this on their open issue at https://github.com/reaviz/reaviz/issues/48

body:
Hi, currently the config with `outputStandalone` is serialized and inlined in the `server.js` file generated to prevent relying on any dependencies in `next.config.js` and prevent unexpected breaking from building to deploying. If the `serverRuntimeConfig` values can not be set during build-time and need to be able to change with the `env` then you can change the inlined config in the `server.js` file to `conf: require('./path/to/next.config.js')` instead. 

Also, to better understand why `serverRuntimeConfig` is being used here, why are you storing the env variable in `serverRuntimeConfig` instead of accessing `process.env.API_URL` in the code directly where needed?
author: ijjk

body:
Hello @ijjk,
Thanks for your feedback. I did try to modify server.js, but as you said, next.config.js has some dependencies, so I couldn't make it work. As for why using serverRuntimeConfig, I honestly don't have an answer. I guess this was a historical config, trying to also support client-side config. I will experiment with using the env directly, thanks.
author: dstoyanoff

body:
> Also, to better understand why `serverRuntimeConfig` is being used here, why are you storing the env variable in `serverRuntimeConfig` instead of accessing `process.env.API_URL` in the code directly where needed?

Hi @ijjk!

I may have a use case for this feature: I manage multiple environments for the same Next.js project with credentials (used server-side only) that can vary between environments, for exemple backend URLs (that are proxied through /api routes) & related API secrets.

`serverRuntimeConfig` hydrated with `process.env.MY_VARIABLES` is very useful in this case as it doesn't require the whole project to be rebuilt + packaged in Docker when deploying the project to all the environments.
author: martinezleoml

body: @martinezleoml you shouldn't need to rebuild when using `process.env.MY_VARIABLES`, the only kind of env variables that should require a rebuild are `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefixed and these should only be used for client-side values. It sounds like this can be achieved without `serverRuntimeConfig` and using the env values directly.
author: ijjk

body:
Possibly, it can be achieved yes. And perhaps it should be a consideration for new projects. 

However, for existing projects that follow the `process.env.MY_VARIABLES` in `serverRuntimeConfig` pattern, require more flexibility. Otherwise, they either must do a major refactor or not be able to use `standalone` at all.

author: ahmet8282

body:
Please refer to the image above. As shown, environment variables not prefixed with `NEXT_PUBLIC_` won't be included in the client-side bundle.

The reason for the fallback showing up otherwise is because of how JavaScript works. If the first value is `undefined`, the second will be evaluated. You should not hardcode secrets in your source code.

What's the other problem you are seeing?

body:
Hi, following your reproduction steps, I neither get a 404 nor a 500. :thinking: Could you attach a minimal reproduction repository?

According to the [URI specs](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-2.3), `..` should be valid and 404 is not needed with a [catch-all route](https://nextjs.org/docs/routing/dynamic-routes#catch-all-routes) as the point of it is to catch any non-existent paths.

What I observe instead is that a `.` or `..` does a redirect.

`http://localhost:3000/test/../package-lock.json` -> `http://localhost:3000/package-lock.json`

`http://localhost:3000/test/./package-lock.json` -> `http://localhost:3000/test/package-lock.json`

I tested this behavior in Google Chrome and Firefox, and both have the same behavior with non-Next.js sites as well:

You can try it:

1. `https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/./33912`  will simply omit the `./`
2. `https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/../33912` will end up in a 404 on `https://github.com/vercel/next.js/33912`

(In fact when I wanted to let the above URLs be as plain text without the code fence `, GitHub simply rewrote those URLs).

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body: Hi, `next/link` should not be used in `_document` as it's meant for client-side transitions and as mentioned in [the docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/custom-document) `_document` is only server-side rendered and event-handlers do not work in this file.

body:
I did some digging, this is because we don't currently pass the Browserslist config to SWC (default compiler over Babel since Next 12), only to PostCSS. So CSS will be correctly targeted, while JS won't.

This is planned to land when this RFC goes out: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227

So please follow that for further information.

Thanks for reporting! :pray:

author: balazsorban44

body:
Having another look, I noticed a few issues:

I noticed you are using `exports`.  You should add `type: "module"` to the `package.json` file to tell Next.js it should be consumed as an ESModule.

Furthermore, your `tsconfig.json` sets `"module": "commonjs"` which will break, even if `type: "module"`. If you want to make a true ESM package, set that to `ES2015` or something higher.

Have a look at the Node.js documentation for more context: https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html

Corporating those changes, I only needed a small additional change to get your setup working.

Somehow this line `  "./server": "./dist/server/index.js"` breaks. changing it to `  "./server": "./dist/server/server.js"` and running `yarn clean && yarn dev`, the page `http://localhost:3000/api/hello` loads correctly.

So there is still a high chance of a bug here, just wanted to share what I've found so far.
author: balazsorban44

body:
The issue is likely related to this line:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/7be6359aff34e0e8c27fe4e16ccd32d6cd0ca5e4/packages/next/build/webpack-config.ts#L880

Poking around with it locally, I could get one of the cases working.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Thanks for engaging!

> I noticed you are using exports. You should add type: "module" to the package.json file to tell Next.js it should be consumed as an ESModule.
> 
> Furthermore, your tsconfig.json sets "module": "commonjs" which will break, even if type: "module". If you want to make a true ESM package, set that to ES2015 or something higher.

I'll consider publishing my module as ESM only, but I vaguely recall running into some issues because of that. Regardless, my expectation is that Next.js should be able to `import` any CommonJS module.

> Somehow this line "./server": "./dist/server/index.js" breaks. changing it to "./server": "./dist/server/server.js" and running yarn clean && yarn dev, the page http://localhost:3000/api/hello loads correctly.

This is changing the semantics of the package. I do want to export `server/index.ts` and `client/index.ts` because those files will import and re-export many modules in addition to `server.ts` and `client.ts`.

> The issue is likely related to this line:

That sounds plausible! It really is based on the directory name, so some Webpack code resolution settings seem like the likely culprit.

bscaspar

body:
Hi, I'm not able to reproduce this with the information provided. Is `notFound: true` being used on the dynamic page, if so is `revalidate` being returned in that case as well? The default behavior when `revalidate` is not returned is to never revalidate as documented [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-props#revalidate). 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/22380829/152613000-4536a8dd-0ddc-4676-8462-c77df9f6252e.mp4
author: ijjk

body:
I see - in our case when `notFound` is returned, the `revalidate` prop is not included.

In my mental model, when using `fallback: 'blocking'` a 404 page would attempt to re-render the page on subsequent requests to the same path. It sounds like I was thinking about it slightly wrong - 404 pages are treated the same way as other statically generated pages and cached for a given path. 

What was also confusing is that this seemed to work before updating to next v12. Perhaps it's just something we didn't catch.
author: bscaspar

body:
`notFound` should be used when your data source (like a CMS) cannot return the data for a certain path. Since the data can be updated by the CMS authors at a later time, `notFound`+`revalidate` allows your site to recover from a 404 without a redeploy.

There is also the following note in the docs:

> `fallback: 'blocking'` will not _update_ generated pages by default. To update generated pages, use [Incremental Static Regeneration](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration) in conjunction with `fallback: 'blocking'`.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-static-paths#fallback-blocking

This holds even if you returned `notFound: true`.

body:
As a temporary solution that doesn't make me skipLibChecks on everything, I am using ignore-bad-ts.  I've added it as a yarn 3 plugin in my project, but you could easily call it via cli

```
npx ignore-bad-ts "node_modules/next/types/index.d.ts"
```

IMPORTANT: the package just adds ts-nocheck to the top of files that match the glob.  So you will want to clean your packages if you install a fixed version of NextJS and remove the ignore-bad-ts calls.  I prefer this though, so that I still get my typechecking for everything else except next.
author: rr-justin-hanselman

body: I don't think we are going to backport patches like this. The reason is that Next.js generally tries to keep breaking changes at a minimum, even between major releases. The only good candidate for backporting is security patches for slightly active older versions. Upgrading to Next 12 solves the issue, with the one caveat that if you want `"skipLibCheck": false`, you might need to install `@types/react-dom` as a dev dependency as well.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Just to be clear, changing a type definition for a still-supported version of NextJS is considered a breaking change?  I'm not sure what all the NextJS eco-system is reliant on, but that seems a little drastic to me.  However, I'm clearly not willing to apply the fix as a PR at this moment, so I'll take your word on it.

If this is the final resolution, for anyone who comes across this and doesn't want to disable all type checking, please see my above workaround and keep an eye out for new NextJS releases that might implicitly fix this. :D
author: rr-justin-hanselman

body:
I might have been unclear. This has been fixed already in Next.js 12 and upwards, no need for PR or any further fix.

What I meant was that it is fairly easy to upgrade between major releases of Next.js, since the breaking changes are kept at a very low level, making it a very stable framework. I did not say that a type fix would be a breaking change, only that we are not implementing patches/fixes/features on older versions of Next.js unless it addresses a security issue.

body:
Sorry @balazsorban44 
The example was incomplete, as I putted it here only for illustration.

I looked into this matter a little more today.
I pinpointed the issue to what version of `react-is` is hoisted by yarn.
You can try with the docker image of this [repo](https://github.com/victorenaud/next-bug-illustration)

While `styled-component` was the only dependency, `react-is@16.13.1` was hoisted and all was working well.
When I added some more dependencies using another version, `react-is@17.0.2` became the hoisted version of `react-is`.

And that broke the docker image.
ie if I launch the docker image and try to access the website, the error `Error: Cannot find module 'react-is'` is shown

If I go back to next.js version `12.0.8`, all is good again.
So I suppose something in `12.0.9` broke the build?
author: VictorEnaud

body: I'll have another look, thanks. I would still recommend having a look at our official example, as it can significantly reduce the size and build time of your image. (Referring to https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/32258)
author: balazsorban44

body:
I found this note in `styled-components` docs: https://styled-components.com/docs/faqs#what-do-i-need-to-do-to-migrate-to-v5

Through this issue: https://github.com/styled-components/styled-components/issues/2874

Adding `"react-is": "^16.8.0"` to dev dependencies seems to solve the issue.


Nonetheless, I simply swapped your `Dockerfile` with our recommended Dockerfile: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/examples/with-docker/Dockerfile

and added the following to `next.config.js`:
```js
  experimental: {
    outputStandalone: true,
  },
```

And the server works fine now, without adding `react-is`!

It looks like there is something in your Dockerfile that breaks it, which I'm unable to pinpoint.

Hope that this helps! Feel free to ping me again if you have more questions!
author: balazsorban44

body:
I tried the recommended Dockerfile, and it worked, thank you!

Moreover, it reduced our containers size by ~50%, which is a nice side-effect. :tada:

body:
Hi, this is because, by default, the Next.js dev server will get rid of entries outside of the watch period. This is 15 seconds by default:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/c051af85289a67856d692cf3db2540495718e87c/packages/next/server/config-shared.ts#L422

You can configure this to be a higher value by the `onDemandEntries` option in `next.config.js`.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/configuring-onDemandEntries
author: balazsorban44

body: Additional comment, we are looking into ways to make `onDemandEntries` unnecessary.

Is there an issue for this already?

Make sure you are actually trying a version `12.0.1` or above, otherwise I'll need a deployed URL I could verify.

Note: opening StackBlitz itself in Chrome 65 fails because it does not support that.
author: balazsorban44

body:
> This should have already been addressed in #30387, introduced in [`12.0.1`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases/tag/v12.0.1) 🤔.

#30387 only checks whether the API exists at all, but in case it does it assumes full functionality. In my case, the problem is that the API exists but is not functional in the way the callee expects. Hence the error message: `Failed to execute 'observe' on 'PerformanceObserver': required member entryTypes is undefined.`
author: jpkempf

body:
> I just checked, and my deployed version at https://34062.vercel.app loads the image and does not reproduce what you are describing:
> 
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18369201/152841048-7b2d5cca-6ec8-4345-bab8-3c753d2abb66.png)
> 
> Make sure you are actually trying a version `12.0.1` or above, otherwise I'll need a deployed URL I could verify.
> 
> Note: opening StackBlitz itself in Chrome 65 fails because it does not support that.

Thanks for going to the trouble and sorry StackBlitz didn't work as I intended! That is interesting to hear that it works for you. I've just reconfirmed in my environment that the repro still produces the error.

The problem might not be with the "normal" version of Chrome, but with the build that I am running in this project. What I'm actually dealing with is a version of [CefSharp](https://github.com/cefsharp/CefSharp/) running as a modal window inside native applications written in C++ and C#. There may differences in the browser engine that are not obvious from the user agent alone.

This also means it is not possible for me to offer an exact, web-based reproduction of the issue, since my runtime environment is rather unusual. I can only describe what I am observing.

Edit: I did make extra sure I'm running Next.js version 12.0.10. 🙂
author: jpkempf

body:
> Next.js supports IE11 and all modern browsers (Edge, Firefox, Chrome, Safari, Opera, et al) with no required configuration.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features

So yes, CefSharp might fall out of direct support. I wonder if adding a polyfill would be enough in your use case then, as you are already doing. Is there a drawback with that solution?

Otherwise, I see that CefSharp have support for much newer Chrome versions: https://github.com/cefsharp/CefSharp/#release-branches

Are you able to upgrade?

If you could give me some minimal steps on how to reproduce the issue with your runtime, I could try to give it a go.
author: balazsorban44

body:
There isn't really a list of "minimal" steps—you would basically have to set up a development environment similar to the one I am using, which requires quite some effort. If you are truly interested, then I would be happy to try and arrange a call for walking you through the behaviour I've described, but this is certainly not something I would expect anyone to be willing to do.

Sadly, the version of CefSharp that is in use depends on whichever version the vendor of the software embedding the browser chooses to go with, and for that reason we are stuck with these old versions in my project. Even if a new version of the software were to get released that uses a newer version of CefSharp, my project would still be obliged to support the older versions for the foreseeable future.

The crude polyfill I have in place is working for now; there are no drawbacks to my use case that I can see. The only problem I can think of is if the image component were to rely more on the output of PerformanceObserver in the future, in which case I might have to spend more effort on mocking the observer's methods and their responses.

I am well aware that my issue is an extreme edge case, and I would not hold it against anyone if they closed this issue. 😅
author: jpkempf

body:
I understand. Unfortunately, I cannot give any guarantees that newer APIs won't be leveraged more in the future, but we generally try to add very little breaking changes over time. Maybe https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227 might help in the future, that would let you utilize Browserslist to transform the code as you wish, potentially including the polyfill for your target automatically.

Another option is https://github.com/fastly/performance-observer-polyfill

I'm going to close this for now then. :+1:
author: balazsorban44

body:
The reason behind this issue is: 
`PerformanceObserver` spec either requires `entryTypes` inside argument, or `type` and `buffered` both (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/PerformanceObserver/observe#parameters). 
However, some older browsers do not acknowledge it and throw error when `entryTypes` is not provided.

body:
This is more of a workaround that is better than your current method. (In my opinion of course)
You could use a fetching data library like SWR also by Vercel.  

```js
useSWR('/api/data', fetcher, { fallbackData: prefetchedData })
```
Where prefetched data would be the applicable props from getServerSideProps 
And then just have an api page that calls the same internal code as getServerSideProps and returns the applicable props.

When you need to update it, you can just call mutate. It will also automatically re-fetch it on window focus after a few seconds (by default)

Here's a link to client side rendering.
https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/client-side

body:
Ok, further investigation this is caused by my pages having a `getStaticProps` defined. I might misunderstand how Next.js work.

My understanding is that `getStaticProps` triggers the page to be statically generated and will be rendered within `_app.tsx` on request after `getInitialProps` has done its thing. Making `_app.tsx` the perfect place to do global stuff like layout and auth.

Although this seems to be wrong. It would seem that adding a `getStaticProps` to a page forces `getInitialProps` to be run exclusively on the frontend for that specific page. 
Is that correct?
author: fANZYo

body: I would recommend the use of getServerSideProps. Looking at your code I saw a typo. getInitialProps receives a context parameter containing response or request. So your context.ctx  doesn't exists since context is already ctx but it does contain res and req. See: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/data-fetching/get-initial-props#context-object

body:
There is no issue with `rewrites`. You are trying to check whether a link is active by using your `rewrites` path compare with the path that shown in the browser, which is `asPath`.

According to `Next.js` useRouter documentation:
- `asPath`: The path (including the query) shown in the browser without the configured basePath or locale.
- `pathname`: Current route. That is the path of the page in /pages, the configured basePath or locale is not included.

Without `rewrites`, there are many cases that this implementation doesn't work as expected. What I would recommend is changing the condition that compare `pathname` which is `router.pathname` and the destination route which is `link.route` in your issue demo.

Adding a `.babelrc` file with `{"presets":["next/babel"]}` could be a temporary solution.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Thanks @balazsorban44 After going back and re-reading the full pull request made by @glaser, I've come to the conclusion that trying to use ApolloServer with NextJS API routes is a really really bad idea. My conclusions:

* apollo-server-micro is obsolete and should not be included in any NextJS examples 
* apollo-server-express is expecting to not be cold started on every request so inappropriate for serverless
* The Next team is to busy to do a proper integration of ApolloServer and the Apollo team does not know how to

Based on this, I'm going to go back to having a separate apollo server and if I want apollo server to be serverless I will follow one of the Apollo teams recommended solutions and not try to shoe horn into API Routes.

I know at one time @ijjk @Timer and @timneutkens were actively involved in keeping the Apollo examples up to date, but that is obviously not the case any more. I suggest Vercel move the demos out of the repo into some archival storage so people like me don't waste a lot of time trying to get them to work and fail.

body:
Hi,

I do see that a chunk shoots up to 1.3MiB on `12.0.9-canary.11`.

```shell
    1.3 MiB [##########]  817.js
   28.0 KiB [          ]  40.js
    8.0 KiB [          ]  863.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  976.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  144.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  font-manifest.json
```

Is there any limitation for you to try `12.0.11-canary.11`? I have tried with that build, and the error is gone.

```shell
   76.0 KiB [##########]  664.js
   16.0 KiB [##        ]  755.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  320.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  730.js
    4.0 KiB [          ]  font-manifest.json
```

My wild guess is that the import in `packages/next/server/web-server.ts`, was bringing unwanted modules.

```ts
import { LoadComponentsReturnType } from './load-components'
```
The current implementation is doing the proper way to bring a type, without bringing the entire module:

```ts
import type { LoadComponentsReturnType } from './load-components'
```

author: brkalow

body:
Hi, so this is currently expected as the page being rewritten to is an [automatically statically optimized page](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization) so it can't know the `asPath` on the server and only knows it on the client. The `asPath` value shouldn't be used inside of the rendering tree until `router.isReady` is true. We might need to document this better cc @molebox 

Also note using `typeof window` in the rendering tree ([related line](https://github.com/BRKalow/next-rewrite-hydration-mismatch/blob/01126e4da785ec0fc29920a63653c0f7c386bd5e/pages/proxied/index.jsx#L19)) will cause hydration issues and needs a `isMounted` check before differing the rendering based on this

author: balazsorban44

body:
I haven't tried canary yet, but I did upgrade to `^12.0.10` and still see the issue.

Specifically it seems to be successfully fetching the css resources when they are included in the html (from the initial server side render, this is CORS exempt) but then client side navigations via router trigger a javascript `fetch` for the css resource, and then the CORS is rejected (javascript triggered `fetch` of css is not CORS exempt).

What's odd to me is that it seems like the `fetch` is somehow omitting the `Origin` header.

S3 typically omits the CORS response headers if the `Origin` request header is absent. If I run `curl -v -H 'Origin: <anything>' <my_cdn_url>` then the response headers include the cross origin headers:

```
< access-control-allow-origin: *
< access-control-allow-methods: GET, HEAD
< access-control-expose-headers: ETag
< access-control-max-age: 3000
```

This confirms that S3/CDN are suitably configured, and suggests that somehow `Origin` is being omitted or removed. The browser network logs, consistent with @kristijorgji's original post, show no CORS response headers, nor any request headers.

I can confirm this issue. We are also using S3 for serving static assets. S3 is correctly adding CORS headers, but it requires `Origin` header to be present. Could there be some asset that's being fetched without an `Origin` header?

![Access to fetch at 'https://areena-web-items.test.ylestatic.fi/0.3.1863-items-fade-bg/_next/static/css/6878b397cb769138.css' from origin 'https://areena-test.yle.fi' has been blocked by CORS policy: No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present on the requested resource. If an opaque response serves your needs, set the request's mode to 'no-cors' to fetch the resource with CORS disabled.](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3000387/161998119-54d76db0-e392-4b78-baa1-2627f374a993.png)

body: I believe this is not next js issue. It is CDN configuration. You need to add the CDN header to add "Access-control-allow-origin" header. This may be a separate bug, but at least related:

I've encountered the same CORS issue as described above - next app is hosted on `stitchfix.com`, assets are on `assets.stitchfix.com`. I see a read from disk, and then a CORS error for the same .css asset initiated from javascript:

![Screenshot_2023-08-31_at_4_19_10_PM](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/assets/1043396/139628f9-0584-41b7-b2c1-c24194f465ee)


This looks to be here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/cc775795890ae9d9587ee978e3573dc94877b1ad/packages/next/src/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1853

The behavior I then see is an infinite refresh of the current page. I'm guessing this is happening a few lines later (`isAssetError`): https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/cc775795890ae9d9587ee978e3573dc94877b1ad/packages/next/src/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L1868-L1880

It's additionally perplexing because I see this which supposedly should prevent a navigation to the same URL: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/cc775795890ae9d9587ee978e3573dc94877b1ad/packages/next/src/shared/lib/router/router.ts#L639-L645

This all started with the introduction of a middleware. I'm not sure if next thinks middleware should run on this asset request, even though it is from a different (sub-)domain.

author: y122972

body:
Hi There, 

We upgraded from Next 12 to Next 14, and started experiencing the same problem in our staging environment, so we have not released to prod yet. 

We did not change our CDN configuration and it was working with its current configuration for several months and Next 12. 

From the symptoms I have investigated when some _next/<some>.js files fails due to CORS, those particular requests are missing the "Origin" request header which when absent makes the CDN config to not sent Access-Control-Allow-Origin in response header. 

I have read all the above comments and my issue is exactly similar with some assets not loading from CDN due to CORS. 

I think there is bug with Next or something new config we don't know. But if I roll back to our old version of Next, the problem goes away.

author: riceboyler

body:
I found one reference which might need the manual `unstable_includeFiles` configuration since the path is defined dynamically:

https://github.com/Mitsunee/fgo-tools/blob/b5886ca7d89e9f052e2bc0164aaf96982cf241a1/pages/events/%5Bslug%5D.jsx#L176

From the docs:

> In those cases, **you can export page configs** props unstable_includeFiles and unstable_excludeFiles respectively. Each prop accepts an array of [globs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glob_(programming)) relative to the project's root to either include or exclude in the trace.

So just to make sure, could you test out `/assets/data/events/**` as a glob pattern? I don't think `assets/data` in itself will match those files you are referencing.

If that works, I still advise being as specific as possible about the inclusions to reduce the output size.

Let me know if that works!

_tested the above pattern on https://globster.xyz/_ with the following data:

```
/assets/data/events/0.yml
/assets/data/events/1.yml
/assets/data/events/2.yml
/assets/data/events/3.yml
/assets/data/login-tickets/4.yml
/assets/data/login-tickets/5.yml
/assets/data/upgrades/6.yml
/assets/data/upgrades/7.yml
/assets/8.svg
/assets/svg/9.svg
```


**UPDATE**

Correction on the above, I re-read our docs, and I believe we can make it slightly easier to understand (I highlighted where it says **page configs**), but the `unstable_includeFiles` is a page config, not a `next.config.js` option, which means you will need to do the following:

In `pages/events/[slug].js`, add the following:

```js
export const config = {
  unstable_includeFiles: ["assets/data/events/*.yml"]
};
```

As you can see below, this will include the desired assets in the standalone app:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18369201/153678370-df57771f-df03-4247-9c16-d984eb007b0e.png)


As a caution, I would like to note that any static assets added **after** the server has started won't be available.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving

body:
Hi @Mitsunee so the above `includeFiles` glob isn't working since it's not a regex but a [minimatch](https://www.npmjs.com/package/minimatch) glob instead which would be good for us to ensure is documented. 

Updating the glob to this should resolve the issue:
```js
export const config = {
  unstable_includeFiles: ["public/**/*.*(svg|ico)"]
}
```

PR updating documentation for this opened here https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/34269

body:
>  routeChangeComplete event for the initial page load, as documented elsewhere

Could you link to this documentation please? :pray: That event is supposed to be called when a page transition has completed.

> Fires when a route changed completely

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/router#routerevents

For the latter, the reason rewrites emits the event is because of the second render as explained here: 
https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization.md#how-it-works

(This is admittedly not in on our [matching docs page](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization#how-it-works) as of writing, since it was recently clarified in #34049 but will be deployed :soon:)

Closing as this is expected.
author: balazsorban44

body:
> > routeChangeComplete event for the initial page load, as documented elsewhere
> 
> Could you link to this documentation please? 🙏 That event is supposed to be called when a page transition has completed.

By "documented elsewhere", I was referring to this issue: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/4662

But to clarify, as I understand it: the expected behavior is for `onRouteChangeComplete` NOT to fire for the initial page load, as discussed in that thread. I was not attempting to suggest that documentation was wrong.

> For the latter, the reason rewrites emits the event is because of the second render as explained here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization.md#how-it-works

If I'm reading this correctly, the flow goes something like this:

* Client loads the page http://localhost:3333
* The initial route of path `/` is loaded with an empty query object, without firing the event
* The rewrite config causes the page to rehydrate with the query object
* The rehydration navigates to the same route path but now with the query object, which fires the route change events

If so, now I understand why it's behaving this way.

> Closing as this is expected.

It may be expected, but is it intuitive or convenient for users of this software?

Some things I don't like about how it behaves right now:

* Not necessarily obvious to a new user who has not developed a deep understanding of the router and how Next config interacts with it
* No simple one-size-fits-all approach to handling page visit tracking (sometimes we have to explicitly grab the initial URL, sometimes we don't)
* No way for a custom component wrapping this logic to be agnostic to the Next config

If you are using Docker, have a look at these:

author: balazsorban44

body:
Thanks for the tips. Following your message; I do remember that we enabled the following Azure option: 'Run from zip', https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service/deploy-run-package

This approach allows us to build the website on an Azure build agent and deploy still within 5 minutes. The alternative was making a zip while keeping the symlinks intact and then extracting this on the deployed app service.

The last approach is not ideal at all. It just is a real pain to use Azure with NextJS. I understand that you can use anything to host but it doesn't seem to be optimized to build on another machine than the actual server on which it will run due to native dependencies to that hardware.

I'll investigate or the ZIP feature is responsible for this and test next/image a bit deeper on Azure. Thanks!
author: aidan-rypens

body:
Thanks @balazsorban44 for the advice!

It indeed is the `WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE` setting on Azure App services ([link](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service/deploy-run-package)) that is causing the issue.

The feature is really neat as it drastically reduces release time on an Azure deployment (In our case 4mins). Using the traditional 'Package deployment using ZIP Deploy' method eliminates the problem but it takes forever (like 15-25minutes) to send the prepared application (it already includes the `node_modules` folder).

**However**, some of the points:
- 'Running directly from a package makes `wwwroot` read-only. Your app will receive an error if it tries to write files to this directory.'
- And in the prepared zip, there is a `.next/cache` folder but no `.next/cache/images`.

Will be testing the advanced feature you mentioned, Automatically Copying Traced Files. It does however also need to run other stuff apart from the NextJS framework.
author: aidan-rypens

body: Thanks for confirming. This sounds like a platform issue then, and not a bug in Next.js. Closing, but feel free to report back how it went. :+1:

body:
I tried to use a code snippet like `typeof fetch === 'function' ? fetch : require('undici').fetch` to avoid it trying to load `undici` when `fetch` is already available but that doesn't work.

The `undici` library is used in a third-party Node.js library that I want to use in a middleware. 

Is there a way to only let it execute `require('undici') when `fetch` is not globally defined so I can use the library in other middleware and 'normal' Next.js API routes?
author: weyert

body:
Hi, this is expected as Middleware does not support Node.js APIs like [`async_hooks`](https://nodejs.org/api/async_hooks.html).

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime#unsupported-apis
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44  but when I use code like `typeof fetch === 'function' ? fetch : require('undici').fetch` why is it still trying to load `undici`-module when fetch is defined?

So it means you can't use any typical packages?
author: weyert

body:
From the [above-linked](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime#unsupported-apis) pages:

> why is it still trying to load undici-module

- Calling require directly is not allowed. Use ES Modules instead

> So it means you can't use any typical packages?

- Node Modules can be used, as long as they implement ES Modules and do not use any native Node.js APIs

In this case, you wouldn't need to use `undici` in the first place, because the environment the code will be run in already has `fetch`.

```
../../next.js/packages/next/dist/build/webpack/loaders/css-loader/src/index.js??ruleSet[1].rules[2].oneOf[8].use[1]!../../next.js/packages/next/dist/build/webpack/loaders/postcss-loader/src/index.js??ruleSet[1].rules[2].oneOf[8].use[2]!./src/styles/index.css
Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: require() of ES Module /path/to/my/repo/components/tailwind.config.js from /path/to/my/repo/components/node_modules/tailwindcss/lib/lib/setupTrackingContext.js not supported.
Instead change the require of tailwind.config.js in /path/to/my/repo/components/node_modules/tailwindcss/lib/lib/setupTrackingContext.js to a dynamic import() which is available in all CommonJS modules.
```
author: ctjlewis

body: Worked around for Tailwind and PostCSS by forcing CJS in the `type: module` project with `.cjs` file extension for `postcss.config.cjs` and `tailwind.config.cjs`.
author: ctjlewis

body:
If anyone is struggling with TailwindCSS + "type: module":

- Rename the tailwind config file to "tailwind.config.cjs"
- Put your config from postcss.config.js as JSON to your package.json: under the key "postcss".
- Remove your postcss.config.js file.
- Remove the .next folder from your project directory to clear the cache and restart

author: madeleineostoja

body:
@damianobarbati 
> @BB-19 is not working for me, what version of next are you using?
> 
> ```shell
> error - ./node_modules/next/dist/build/webpack/loaders/css-loader/src/index.js??ruleSet[1].rules[2].oneOf[8].use[1]!./node_modules/next/dist/build/webpack/loaders/postcss-loader/src/index.js??ruleSet[1].rules[2].oneOf[8].use[2]!./src/styles/index.css
> /Users/damians/Desktop/predator/tailwind.config.cjs:29
> export {};
> ^^^^^^
> 
> SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'export'
>     at compileFunction (<anonymous>)
> Ready on http://localhost:80
> ```

I was receiving the same error myself when using `ts-node` with a custom Next.js server. To resolve this, I added `tailwind.config.cjs` to the ignore option of ts-node. You can do this through the `--ignore` option or `ignore` field in ts-node config in the tsconfig.json file. For example, I now have:
```
...,
"ts-node": {
  "esm": true,
  "ignore": ["node_modules", "tailwind.config.cjs"]
}
```

The reason why "export {}" is added is explained [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65553572/typescript-adds-plain-export-statement). Namely that, 
> the compiler must emit the export statement in order to ensure that the compiled file is also a Module.

This might only be the case if you set `"module": "esnext"` in your tsconfig file, which compiles TypeScript files to ESM modules. You might also have `"type": "module"` in your package.json if that's the case, as I do.

My guess for why adding this to ts-node's ignore field is required is that normally with just `tsc` TypeScript wouldn't compile `tailwind.config.cjs`, and I'm guessing that postcss/tailwind dynamically `require`s it (perhaps something like `require('tailwind.config.' + extension)` or something like that, which TypeScript can't compile since it can't know what the file is at compile-time), so it would just require it normally and work fine. But with ts-node, since it is now compiling at runtime, the dynamic require would now cause tailwind.config.cjs to be compiled and thus have the `export {}` line added. Adding the file to the `ignore` field stops it from doing so.

body: @BB-19 you saved me, but why does this work?
author: DannyPark1

body:
> @BB-19 you saved me, but why does this work?

PostCSS and Tailwind do not support ESM config files. You must rename them to .cjs.

There's no need to move postcss config to package.json and I don't recommend that.

author: Triment

body:
> If anyone is struggling with TailwindCSS + "type: module":
> 1.Rename the tailwind config file to "tailwind.config.cjs"
> 2.Put your config from postcss.config.js as JSON to your package.json: under the key "postcss".
> 3.Remove your postcss.config.js file.
> 4.Remove the .next folder from your project directory to clear the cache and restart

[**@BB-19**](url), No need to rename `tailwind.config.js` to `tailwind.config.cjs`. The problem is `postcss.config.js`, remove it and add the config to `package.json`. For example

```json
{
...
  "type": "module",
  "postcss": {
    "plugins": {
      "tailwindcss": {},
      "autoprefixer": {}
    }
  },
...
}
```

For me, Yarn Berry with PnP Strict, the solution for convert `next` project from CommonJS to ESModule:

1. Add or update `package.json` with "type": "module"
2. Remove `postcss.config.js` and apply it to `package.json` with "postcss" json format, see above example
3. Update `next.config.js` and `tailwind.config.js` to ESM format
4. Add "pnpEnableEsmLoader: true" to `.yarnrc.yml`
5. Clear Cache `yarn cache clean && rm -rf yarn.lock && yarn install && yarn build`

Hope it help to following guys 🤗

author: vishnupb123

body:
> PostCSS and Tailwind do not support ESM config files

@ctjlewis This is not true anymore, both support ESM:

- https://github.com/postcss/postcss-load-config/issues/230
- https://github.com/postcss/postcss-cli/issues/387
- https://tailwindcss.com/docs/configuration#using-esm-or-type-script

Tailwind CSS config files can also be written in ESM syntax with TypeScript - and this [already works now with Next.js](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/create-next-app/templates/app-tw/ts/tailwind.config.ts).

As far as I can tell, this is a problem with Next.js or some other dependency in between.
author: karlhorky

body:
In the meantime, before Next.js supports ESM in PostCSS config files, I've opened the following PR to rename `postcss.config.js` to `postcss.config.cjs` in `create-next-app`, so that there is one fewer problem requiring manual intervention when someone wants to add `"type": "module"` to their `package.json`:

- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/58380
author: karlhorky

body:
Just upgraded to `next@14.0.4` and [`postcss-load-config@5.0.2`](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-load-config/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md#502-2022-05-23) and confirmed that the bug still remains, ESM and TypeScript PostCSS formats are still not supported:

- `postcss.config.mjs` (or `postcss.config.js` with ESM in `"type": "module"` projects)
- `postcss.config.ts` [TypeScript config also supported by PostCSS](https://github.com/postcss/postcss-load-config/issues/230#issuecomment-1179718340)

body:
One thing that is **NOT** yet supported is [the `postcss.config.ts` file format supported by `postcss-load-config`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/postcss-load-config#postcssrcjs-or-postcssconfigjs):

```
➜  postcss-esm-and-ts-configs git:(main) ✗ mv postcss.config.mjs postcss.config.ts
➜  postcss-esm-and-ts-configs git:(main) ✗ pnpm dev

> postcss-esm-and-ts-configs@0.1.0 dev /Users/k/p/postcss-esm-and-ts-configs
> next dev

   ▲ Next.js 14.2.0-canary.26
   - Local:        http://localhost:3000

 ✓ Ready in 1666ms
 ○ Compiling / ...
 ✓ Compiled / in 1648ms (523 modules)
 ○ Compiling /favicon.ico ...
 ✓ Compiled /favicon.ico in 793ms (530 modules)
^C
```

It looks like the compilation works, but this results in an unstyled application.

But since this was not the main request by @ctjlewis in this issue, and appeared later in the issue comments, it's probably best to open a new issue for this.
author: karlhorky

body:
One thing that probably could be still improved here is altering `create-next-app` to create `postcss.config.mjs` file with ESM format instead of [the `postcss.config.cjs` file that is currently created](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/58380).

I opened a PR for this here:

- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/63380

author: unitof

body: I am trying to blend between the blurred placeholder and the final image so I stacked an with blurDataURL as src, upon an from next/image. After doing that the page started to crash, at the exact moment when the was mounted and starts to load. (the blurred placeholder was always mounted) And if I remove the placeholder and everything works fine. I think that next/image uses blurhash for blurred placeholders, which are basically SVG files showing the blurry result. Safari seems to consume a lot of memory displaying those images, adding them somehow exceeds the memory limit and the page crashes.

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body:
In addition to the problem described above, I also had been talking about another problem with @huozhi for the past couple days, which is one where every single `Link` click, even if it points to a completely different route, causes the page to go empty for a split second.

After digging a bit deeper, it turns out that, in both cases, `display: none` is applied to the root elements by Next.js/React, so the root problem might actually be the same for both.

Note that the screenshot I'm showing below is from Vercel's [next-react-server-components](https://github.com/vercel/next-react-server-components) example, so there's no code present in there that could cause these styles to be added. It must be Next.js or React.

![CleanShot 2022-02-17 at 12 08 21@2x](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6170607/154469621-8c42e76d-67fd-4cdf-8e00-73d1d324dd7e.png)

Keep in mind, you talk about your own use case, but this issue was opened by someone else, with their own reproduction, being specific about `monaco-editor`. https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34501#issuecomment-1046771478 is unfortunately insufficient information to infer that your use case is related. Could you please add your own reproduction? That's always the biggest help to us resolving an issue. :pray:

Part of my comment from https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/31692#issuecomment-1063046912 still applies here. When using a custom webpack config, you deliberately try to override Next.js rules that might have changed between 11 and 12, so without a proper reproduction, it is hard to tell how to accommodate for that.

Let's reopen then for further investigation.

body:
Hi, thanks for reporting! Although not exactly the same issue, I would like to close this in favor of https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/13231 which also addresses using the dev server (more precisely Hot Module Replacement) in IE11.

I can probably tell that unfortunately, this is not a top priority, as IE11 is considered a legacy browser.

That said, we do [support it as a build target](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features) and if you find issues with that please report it, but even our default target is changing (so IE11 support would become opt-in as outlined here: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227). As the ecosystem moves away from IE11, it makes less sense to put much effort into making the dev server work in IE11 either.
author: balazsorban44
parlay96

body: You don't have to develop in IE11 to make your site IE11 compatible. `next build` will compile your code into an IE11 compatible bundle. As long as the packages you are using are also compatible, you should be good. If you find an issue with the Next.js bundle, please open a separate issue, attaching your code/a reproduction repository so we can have a look. Unfortunately, screenshots are not good enough reproductions. :pray:
author: balazsorban44

body: You mean I don't need to pay attention to the next dev development environment? Automatically match IE after next build?
author: parlay96

body:
Yes. Next.js adds the required polyfills as stated here https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features

You must still make sure that the packages you Wil be using are compatible as well, but from the Next.js side you should be good. 👍

body:
The rest of your comment about `path-to-regexp` makes sense, but could I ask for some clarity on how one would go about retrieving the page / route manifest from within a custom middleware?

I've noted anecdotally that `request.page` is not reliable, in that for some paths that I **know** are served by Next I am getting `{ name: undefined, params: undefined }`. For this reason I was about to rewrite my own `path-to-regexp` style logic anyway, which is how I stumbled upon this issue.
author: tavvy

body:
That's good to know @tavvy as it confirms that this feature is not as useful as initially expected. What I mean referring to `path-to-regexp` is that the feature simply relies on checking against all dynamic routes and usually a user would use it check wether it matches or not a _specific page_. Being that the case one can transform, i.e. `/dashboard/:slug` to a regexp and check against `request.nextUrl.pathname` to see if it is matching or not. Within that case one could also discard static file matches.

We can also add a snippet for a helper in the error link to actually make it worth with the exact Next.js page route like `pages/dashboard/[slug]`.
author: javivelasco

body:
Thanks @javivelasco and I can see both the problem and the solution you are proposing now.

Perhaps our use case is a little unique so let me explain as it may be a valid use case of the feature;

We use `rewrites` to fallback to a legacy system. Allowing incremental adoption of Next.js.
`_middleware` runs before **everything** so that includes before rewrites / redirects (although this behaviour differs between `vercel dev` and when actually deployed ... thats one for another day).

We would like a "global" root level middleware to handle session setting. But we **only** want to do this for the Next.js handled pages, when we rewrite to the legacy system it performs its own session handling that we do not want to clash with.

The easiest and most intuitive way to achieve this was to stick an "early exit" handler at the top of the middleware.

```typescript
if (!request.page.name) return NextResponse.next();

// ... session setting logic 
```

Im sure there are different variations on this usage. Basically it is useful to have _some_ kind of indicator that the page is a known route to the Next.js app.

Hence my question about how I might access the page manifest from within the middleware to construct my own `path-to-regexp` matching logic, otherwise I'll need to somehow pass the page manifest into the middleware at build time.

---

**You could just scope your `_middleware` to each page rather than have a root level one?**
Yes we _could_, but its a lot of duplication. And I do envisage requirements soon to perform different logic in the root `_middleware` depending on whether or not its a Next.js page or a path going to be picked up by the fallback rewrite. 

**What if you do the inverse and match on a known fallback route?**
It won't work if you have a catchall: `{source: '/:path*', destination: 'https://legacy-system.com:path*'}` as every incoming request would match.
author: tavvy

body:
> although this behaviour differs between vercel dev and when actually deployed ... thats one for another day
Ah ha! That little discrepancy had me chasing my tail for far too long!

Should issues that are specific to Vercel deployment rather than Next be reported here?

My Vercel issue: https://github.com/vercel/vercel/issues/7452
author: jake-nz

body:
I've realised that Next itself needs to know which page will be matched in middleware.

Given folder structure like this:
* pages
  * index.tsx
  * page.tsx
  * _middleware.ts
  * [slug]
    * index.tsx
    * _middleware.ts
  
When a user requests /page `request.page.name = '/page'`
When a user requests /page2 `request.page.name = '/[slug]'`
Next needs to know this so that it can decide whether to run `[slug]/_middleware.ts` or not.

My suggestion would be to keep `request.page` because it needs to be computed and it's useful to pass on to middleware. Having some indication that there is a static file that matches would be a more useful solution.
author: jake-nz

body:
I encounted the same behaviour
- from what I can tell the "pages" themselves are regenerated 
- but the assotiated jsons are not 
- so the page `/`  is updated, but `de.json` is not (we use i18n)
- same for individual article routes `/test` updates, but not `test.json`.
- so you only ever get the new version on first page load, but never on client side route changes

This also means that as on-demand-isr you'll usually want to omit revalidate that the jsons never get updated.

body:
Hi, thanks for reporting.

Just to understand your use case, in which scenario would you like to run the dev server in a CI environment?

Otherwise, I can see that `npm run build` will also fail as it also tries to touch that file, but I think it should be safe to assume that the `tsconfig.json` file should have been correctly configured before it reaches CI.

Next.js sets/overrides certain options for the most optimal output, and I can see no immediate changes to fix your problem, other than pushing a commit with the updated `tsconfig.json` file.

We could make the error message nicer, but in reality, it really should have been caught already in the development process.

Do you have a proposal for a fix/change?
author: balazsorban44
tgolsson

body:
Thank you for the extra info!

Here is the reasoning of why Next.js does this currently:

> Next.js sets/overrides certain options for the most optimal output

I'll discuss with the others if we should either allow continuing the build with a warning that tells the user the output might not be optimal, or we should still crash but add a more clear error message with a link to a page that gives an actionable way to fix it.

body:
Hi, thank you for reporting this!

I could identify the problem and mentioned it to the relevant team. Luckily, this is not a bug in Next.js, only that our examples should be updated to reflect #33410.

You should see an error in your Vercel Dashboard that points to this page:
https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/middleware-relative-urls#possible-ways-to-fix-it

I deployed a version that works here: https://balazsorban-34566-balazsorban.vercel.app/

The code changes needed are in the two `_middleware` files respectively:

```js
const destination = req.nextUrl.clone()
destination.pathname = `/home/${bucket}` // or /marketing
const res = NextResponse.rewrite(destination)
```

body:
i guess your problem is that you a hook `useScreenType` to change the layout, which won't work with Server Side Rendering.  So your server does not know what device the client is and will allways render one version. 

That could lead to hydration problems like props beeing assigned to the wrong element.

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

body:
This seems to be already reported here: https://github.com/expo/expo-cli/issues/4195

The [Expo docs](https://docs.expo.dev/guides/using-nextjs) also explicitly ask to report Next.js related issues on their repo:

> Please open any issues related to Next.js with Expo at [expo-cli/issues](https://github.com/expo/expo-cli/issues).

There is a chance that Expo does not work with Next.js 12 yet, let's see what they report on that issue to verify.

From the looks of it, the `@expo/next-adapter` package still assumes webpack 4, which has been removed in Next.js 12: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12#breaking-changes

Found this relevant thread also: https://github.com/expo/expo-cli/issues/4066

Also `@expo/next-adapter` is locked in with Next.js 11 as an acceptable peer dependency: https://github.com/expo/expo-cli/blob/c73681f811e254bfaf40e1459b62cfd3e9a15167/packages/next-adapter/package.json#L51

I think we should wait until the Expo team comes out with an official statement that Expo is compatible with Next.js 12 and updates their adapter. When that's done, we could update our example. :+1:

body:
Honestly I have been having inconsistent results with this.
Sometimes the envs are available with the prefix `NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE` and sometimes you have to access them without the prefix `DEFAULT_LOCALE`... but sometimes it changes.

I dont know if its a caching thing, not sure what it is but today its had me confused as heck.

Today this is what i saw:
**.env.local**
```
NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE=fr
```

**next.config.js**
in next config you can access this as a non public env
```
async redirects() {
  console.log(process.env.DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- fr
  console.log(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- undefined
}

But then after some rebuilds i noticed it the other way around:
async redirects() {
  console.log(process.env.DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- undefined
  console.log(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- fr
}
```

**some_page.js**
in getStaticProps which the documentation says only runs on the server, the node env is undefined but the public is defined.... confusing.
```
export const getStaticProps: GetStaticProps = async ({ params, preview }) => {
  console.log(process.env.DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- undefined
  console.log(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- fr
}

and then it reversed on another run
export const getStaticProps: GetStaticProps = async ({ params, preview }) => {
  console.log(process.env.DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- fr
  console.log(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_DEFAULT_LOCALE); <-- undefined
}
```

I dont understand the differences here but now it seems to be consistent. I could be going crazy.
Im on Next 11.1.2

body:
React Server Components have the constraint that they cannot have interactivity, eg. state/effects/event listeners since they are only executed once-per request.

https://github.com/josephsavona/rfcs/blob/server-components/text/0000-server-components.md#capabilities--constraints-of-server-and-client-components

We are exploring ways to read data like cookies to cover your use case in the future.
author: balazsorban44

body: So currently there is no way to fetch logged-in user's data with server component?
author: ghost

body:
So far we didn't have those official utils in Next.js yet, cookie reader is relying on server context work which is still ongoing on React.

In the short term to get cookies and do data fetching, we recommend `getServerSideProps`.

body:
Hi, `const` is supported in IE11, and technically it's not ES5 compatibility we provide, but IE11 support.

https://caniuse.com/?search=const

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/supported-browsers-features

Do you have a reproduction that breaks in IE11 because of this?
author: balazsorban44

body:
Hi @balazsorban44 , thanks for your feedback.

According to https://caniuse.com/?search=const it seems that `const` is only partially supported, according to the note "Not supported in for-in and for-of loops". Which means that every client code which uses `const` and/or `let` (partial support) are being transpiled back to ES5 variant.

So it seems pretty odd that from a recent release (we have recently updated to 12.1.0) server code is being included in the client build as not transpiled. 
To be clear, it does not break anything for now for IE11 but I think it is an anti-pattern and could leverage some problems in the future. I believe that, if some files are being imported in both client and server they should be extracted to a common place.

We have been using `es-check es5 ` on CI steps to make sure a node dependency does not break anything, and if some dependency fails to support es5 it is being added with `next-transpile-modules`.
If you decide not to create an es5 safe build, then we may have to blindly remove this step.
author: vitorhsb

body:
Thanks for the extra context. :+1:

The imported server file exports a single object and is not used in any for loops, so it really should be safe:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/dc208054337127fa2e740d7630976e8d57fa9472/packages/next/server/image-config.ts#L52-L63

> could leverage some problems in the future

I'm curious if you have an example. I think the industry is moving away from IE11 (which as mentioned isn't even an issue here), so it's less likely that this could introduce an issue in the future.

If you target IE11 users specifically, you could let the CI check test for IE11 features, instead of ES5, as seemingly there is no 100% overlap

In reality though, if something would break in IE11 and is reported as a bug in Next.js, we would obviously release a fix immediately, as we do intend to support IE11 currently, and even in the future, we will let it be an opt-in target. See our RFC: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227

That said, I agree it does not hurt to do what you propose, so I'll leave this issue open as a potential improvement opportunity. We should likely move the file as you suggested from the `server` folder and make sure it's included uniformly in the transpilation process.
author: balazsorban44

body:
As mentioned in the RFC https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/33227, "Next.js currently targets [ES5](https://www.w3schools.com/js/js_es5.asp) for JS output". This was somehow my initial presumption to create this kind of issue.

For the moment we have turned our es-check CI step from an error to a warning and will manually check for validation errors. We also have a simple e2e test targeting IE11 to check minimal functionality by loading login page and login action.

Does Next.js have e2e tests agains IE11?

body:
This preflight request is needed so the client knows how to respond after the page transition. Middleware runs before any data fetching methods as well.

It is not supposed to be slow though, so could you maybe show your code so we can determine where the performance issue might be?

Note that we are working on failing on preflight request errors gracefully: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34199
author: balazsorban44

body: For even more context, by default, the preflight request is made when the Link to the other page enters the viewport, so by the time you click it, it should already have been executed/finished. There is a high chance that you are doing an expensive request inside the Middleware (but even that is cached in production so should be fast), so seeing your code would be very helpful.
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44 Thanks for the response. The thing is the main application code I can't share here, but here's a reproduction of the issue: https://codesandbox.io/s/empty-sun-ns5764
