![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/35926159/155073158-c59bea7a-32e0-4b51-801b-e18d06601921.png)

In dev it takes >500-1000ms for an empty page, in production for a slightly heavier request there is also a significant delay for the preflight HEAD request.
author: deve-sh

body:
I get 502 Bad gateway on your reproduction.

Do note that the provided reproduction uses `req.page` which is due deprecation https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34521

For performance debugging, using development (`next dev`), or even CodeSandbox is not ideal. You should test it on a production environment like Vercel, which uses Edge Functions to ensure fast response for Middleware. See a live demo at https://vercel.com/features/edge-functions (Note this is only to showcase an alternative to CodeSandbox. You can choose whatever optimized deployment environment that supports Next.js and Middleware.)

Are you doing any expensive fetch/database calls from your `_middleware`?
author: balazsorban44
deve-sh

body:
Could you please attach your code? :pray: That would be very helpful to further the investigation.

May I ask where you are hosting your site?

CodeSandbox is running a dev server of Next.js, so it is not a good example here, as it isn't optimized for performance and the longer response times can be explained by page compilation happening before the page transition.

I don't see how #33276 is related to slow Middleware calls.
author: balazsorban44
deve-sh

body:
So after discussing this, based on your comment:

> We are hosting on a private cluster on AWS servers.

This is likely an issue with your infrastructure. Next.js does not have a guarantee for fast responses if the underlying infra isn't optimized.

Keep in mind that hosting providers like Vercel do not run your Middleware on AWS Lambda, in order to always have a fast response (by avoiding cold starts, etc). You will have to open a ticket with them to ask if there is a way to eliminate slow responses for your case.

Also again, make sure that you do not test performance in the development environment.

author: smulikHakipod

body:
It looks like the leak is related to when the devtool is open. Devtools keeps the source code of evaluated code alive as it can be displayed in the Sources panel. There seems to be a related Chrome bug:

https://bugs.chromium.org/p/chromium/issues/detail?id=1295659

Try avoiding using devtools for memory dumping. You could for example use `heapdump` instead which allows you to create snapshots without devtools. Also, make sure to use the latest Node.js version.

author: lerot

body:
I think I traced that down to a memory leak in node.js introduced with node.js 16.0.0 with that commit:

https://github.com/nodejs/node/commit/bf2f2b74faeeb5ad7fc8e483a80bae3064e3e717#diff-c1d48dc599e8281b0be28ab449ea52917f6d4cc0578adb61ae99c631078b1a41R387

I tried with node.js 14.19.0 and it doesn't seem to have this leak.

The commit adds an object (`{ importModuleDynamically: ... }`) into a `callbackMap` for every executed function by the vm mode (which is internally used by the CommonJS Module loader).
Technically this `callbackMap` is a WeakMap, but it doesn't seem to cleanup the key.
The function that is passed as `importModuleDynamically` holds on the `func` variable, which keeps the source code of the function alive.

Here the latest version of the code in question: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/45b5ca810a16074e639157825c1aa2e90d60e9f6/lib/vm.js#L352-L383

But why is the key (`cacheKey`) not GC'ed. The `cacheKey` seem to be a plain js object created here: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/45b5ca810a16074e639157825c1aa2e90d60e9f6/src/node_contextify.cc#L1192-L1205

And there is also a C++ `CompiledFnEntry` which holds on the `cacheKey` object.
For the heapdumps it looks like this C++ object is a root object. I really unsure, but maybe one has to call `entry->Detach()` to avoid it being a root object. It's weakly attached to the Script object, and I think it should only be hold by that one...

If that is true the memory leak is maybe related to this commit https://github.com/nodejs/node/commit/89e4b36e62978f54b2e33b4bce8197072dbe8af1
which makes `CompiledFnEntry` a `BaseObject`.

@addaleax @Trott could it be that `CompiledFnEntry` leaks memory? Do one have to call `entry->Detach()` to make it only be hold weakly by the script?
author: sokra

body:
Here are bit more info from the heapdump:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1365881/155134637-7a79869a-7ec3-4088-85e3-6d7ebffbb255.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1365881/155134842-94e35bec-d26a-4ce3-b671-6f8e72ddb78f.png)

The recursive trace looks also a bit suspicious to me, but not sure. Maybe `CompiledFnEntry` and `cacheKey` hold on each other recursively and that can't be handled because it's between C++ and JS object, I don't know...

body: Next.js already supports ES modules, out of the box. What's the use case for `esm`?
author: balazsorban44

body:
I see this has also been reported at https://github.com/standard-things/esm/issues/908

Note, the `esm` package has not been updated since 2019 so it might simply be outdated.

I suggest either waiting for an answer on https://github.com/standard-things/esm/issues/908 or try achieving what you do without `esm`. If the package you are using is ESM and configured correctly, Next.js should already handle it. Read more here: https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html

If you are unsure why a configuration does not work even though you think it should, please open a bug report with a reproduction so we can look into it :pray:
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44 Thanks for the quick response!

Didn't realise there was already an issue on `esm`, apologies.

The use case (which is indeed not reflected in the example) is using ES modules in the custom server and some of its imported, un-transpiled internal modules.

A workaround would be to transpile all of that code but we'd of course prefer to avoid it.

Will check on `esm` then👍
author: danielzlotnik

body:
Thanks for understanding. indeed, the server.js file technically falls outside of Next.js handling.

As a sidenote, we are working on supporting `"type" : "module"` at the app level https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34412, which would then make `esm` obsolete, as all maintained versions of Node.js have native ESM support nowadays.


But more importantly it would be interesting to know the requirements for a custom server in the first place.
author: balazsorban44

body:
@danielzlotnik @balazsorban44 I encounted the issue with Next.js and created the ticket in esm. I can see now that I am not the only one that has this problem now 😄

I tried replacing the dynamic import quickly directly in the file, but it was a bigger task.

I can say that `"type" : "module"` is not the happy ending either, as the module resolution changes
See node --experimental-specifier-resolution=node
https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#customizing-esm-specifier-resolution-algorithm
Combine that with TypeScript and outputting ES Modules and you have to be a literal expert.

So I am stuck on `11.1.3-canary.41` which is the last one that worked for us 😅

Because if I upgrade then Prisma will not work, because the compiler can't treeshake out the prisma dependency from the page as it is only used in getserversideprops.

Providing that extra info helped, thanks @kevinsimper. I can confirm that this has actually worked up until `11.1.3.-canary.77`.

Here is the release notes: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases/tag/v11.1.3-canary.77

It's revolving around bumping the minimal target to a Node.js version that supports ESM, so it sounds very relevant. I believe specifically this PR was the relevant change that broke `esm`: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/29935 that changes how the next.config.js file is loaded.


Actually, not providing a `next.config.js` would still let work `esm` with Next 12. Let me ask the others about this.

> Thanks for understanding. indeed, the server.js file technically falls outside of Next.js handling.
> 
> As a sidenote, we are working on supporting `"type" : "module"` at the app level #34412, which would then make `esm` obsolete, as all maintained versions of Node.js have native ESM support nowadays.
> 
> But more importantly it would be interesting to know the requirements for a custom server in the first place.

@balazsorban44 we used quite a few express middlewares (E.G prometheus, jaeger-tracing, etc..), but thankfully we can now migrate them to the newly introduced Next middleware.

Indeed the path we took was getting rid of the custom server and avoid the problem altogether.
author: danielzlotnik

body:
@kevinsimper could you provide a small reproduction?

I'm wondering what the fate of this issue should be, given the OP does not need a custom server anymore, and `esm` hasn't been updated since 2019... :thinking:

body:
@balazsorban44 I have run into this issue when using the Vercel build API. The issue happens when you're trying to run a piece of code on an edge server. The edge server is running the code in a node VM context which doesnt have dynamic imports configured. That leads to the ERR_VM_DYNAMIC_IMPORT_CALLBACK_MISSING error.

```
TypeError [ERR_VM_DYNAMIC_IMPORT_CALLBACK_MISSING]: A dynamic import callback was not specified.
    at importModuleDynamicallyCallback (node:internal/modules/esm/utils:272:9)
    at $ (user-source.js:19:1249)
    at i (user-source.js:19:1624)
    at Object.g (user-source.js:19:1755)
    at user-source.js:39:28
    at At (edge-functions-bridge-runtime.js:6:8337)
    at edge-functions-bridge-runtime.js:7:16822
    at AsyncLocalStorage.run (node:async_hooks:346:14)
    at Jt (edge-functions-bridge-runtime.js:7:2692)
    at edge-functions-bridge-runtime.js:7:16768
```

body:
Hello again,

After playing with the above, a far more SERIOUS issue has emerged and I would like your help. Image "priority" option is not working always, meaning that it does not always preload the Image putting the corresponding "preload" tag in document's head. 
That happens if instead of including directly the Image component in my page, I import a component which then includes Image.
for example when I have:

<Carousel imageLocation="/image1.jpg".../>
and Inside the Carousel component I have: <Image src={imageLocation} priority .../>

the Image does NOT preload. It only preloads if I have <Image src="image1.jpg" priority .../> DIRECTLY inside my page. 
Is there any known issue that I am not aware about? Also, should I open a new issue for the above case?
Thanks

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

body:
Not sure I fully understand, but are you trying to develop a Next.js app from within an iframe?

localStorage and sessionStorage are only allowed in the same domain, so if your iframe is embedded in a different domain then the Next.js server, it might produce the mentioned issue. This is not a Next.js bug, but how the above-mentioned Web APIs operate. Make sure your iframe embeds a url on the same domain.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Hi @balazsorban44. I'm not sure I fully understand your question either :) My repro speaks for itself, doesn't it? I'm simply loading a basic Next.js app inside an iframe, and it throws an exception from its internal methods. I.e. I am not trying to use the Web APIs from a cross-domain - if I were to do that, I would wrap them inside a try/catch block.

Is there something in my repro that doesn't illustrate the issue?
https://rightsize-store.000webhostapp.com/next_js_test.html (run in Chrome with 3rd party cookies disabled)

I am building an app with Next.js that can be embedded by other websites as an iframe.
author: artooras

body:
> Failed to read the 'localStorage' property from 'Window': Access is denied for this document.

This error is not thrown by Next.js, but by the browser. Disabling third-party cookies also block access to `localStorage` and `sessionStorage`.

> The request violates a policy decision, ... For example, the user may have their browser configured to deny permission to persist data for the specified origin.

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Window/localStorage#exceptions

So you explicitly deny access to APIs that the Next.js dev server relies on.

In production, this won't be a problem unless you explicitly use one of these APIs, as the production build of Next.js does not rely explicitly on `localStorage` or `sessionStorage`
author: balazsorban44

body:
Did some digging, and the only place we reference `sessionStorage` in development is this file: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/packages/next/client/dev/error-overlay/hot-dev-client.js

We use `sessionStorage` to make the full refresh notification modal dismissable (only show up once).

I'll check with the rest of the team about the expectations here. :+1:

author: github-actions

body:
There is unfortunately no quick fix, other than picking a different name for your variable.

Creating a `module` variable hides the normal `module` variable that is used by the HMR react-refresh runtime.

- We can't rename the normal `module` since that is used for CommonJS, e.g. `module.exports = something`
- We could rename the `module` variable via SWC, but that's a bit more involved
- We could lint for `module` when used as a variable name and disallow that.
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44 that makes sense!

Indeed, quite some work involved otherwise, linting would probably be the way to go.

body:
Further investigation shows:

1. `swc` did consume the input source map
2. Because `swc` expects the source file name in input source map to be different from the input file name (`swc`'s input source map handling was tested on `vue-loader`, which passes a different file name into webpack loader chain), the source code in input source map got overridden by source code produced by `coffee-loader`, so the source code shows in error info is wrong.
3. Only a handful positions got correctly mapped back to input source map by `swc`. However, it's just a coincidence that `test()` got mapped to `(1:0)`. The exact reason is still unclear. 
 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1330321/159104786-a5c65354-1f90-48b8-94ed-8a75f6c673c8.png)

(This is after fixing the source code overridden issue)
author: yume-chan

body:
Filed an issue to upstream about the wrong `sourcesContent`.

Also tried pipe the output of `coffee-loader` into standalone `swc`, the source map works correctly. Not sure whether it has been fixed in newer `swc` version, or `next-swc` has extra transformation that doesn't produce correct source map info.

body:
Ended up here because of a similar error but in my case the error message is a 404 (I went in and added the for path part)
```
Error: Error: Invalid response 404 for http://localhost:3000/posts/my-first-post
    at unstable_revalidate (.../node_modules/next/dist/server/api-utils/node.js:228:15)
```
The 404 for this path makes sense because it really should have a subdomain (e.g. http://chris.localhost:3000/posts/my-first-post). Digging further into this `node.js` file, the problem is here,
```js
async function unstable_revalidate(urlPath, req, context) {
    ...
    const baseUrl = context.trustHostHeader ? `https://${req.headers.host}` : `http://${context.hostname}:${context.port}`;
   ...
}
```
If `context.trustHostHeader` is `false`, like in this case, it creates a url that omits a possible subdomain. When deployed, everything works correctly because it evaluates to `true` and uses the first url template.

author: balazsorban44

body:
Here is a simplified version for our consent management (broken version):

```html
<!-- _document.tsx -->
<script>
  window.loadOnConsent = (listOfScripts) => {}; // a script loader
</script>
```

```tsx
// _app.tsx
<Head>
  <script dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: `window.loadOnConsent(${JSON.stringify(getScriptsThatNeedConsent(config))})` }} />
</Head>
```

I know there is the `next/script` component. For us, it is important that we load the consent script as fast as possible. That's why we optimize the loading + execution timings as much as possible. We do this "outside" of react/next and communicate with a global api.
author: bobaaaaa

body:
For this exact case there is the `beforeInteractive` strategy of `next/script`. Why wouldn't that work? :thinking: 

> Scripts that load with the beforeInteractive strategy are injected into the initial HTML from the server and run before self-bundled JavaScript is executed. This strategy should be used for any critical scripts that need to be fetched and executed before the page is interactive.

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/script#beforeinteractive
author: balazsorban44

body: @balazsorban44 it has nothing to do with `next/script` :). I only mentioned it because it is the nextjs-standard answer for inlining javascript. But next/script did not work for us.
author: bobaaaaa

body:
It is, because it gives the guarantees for your requirements. I don't understand why it would not work, as the mentioned strategy is optimized for your use case.

But to make sure if we have a different recommendation, I'll ask the rest of the team. :+1:
author: balazsorban44

body:
Here are some reasons why `next/script` did not work for us:

1. `next/script` did not work in `_document.tsx`
2. inlining code with `next/script` == writing code in strings. This works okay for some configs, but not for a lot of functions.
3. you can not easily test code in strings
4. inlined code will also land in the main bundle (waste of bytes)

I have a wishlist for `next/script` :)

1. works in `_document.tsx`
2. `<Script webpackEntryPoint="./path-to-my-script.ts" />`
author: bobaaaaa

body:
**Google Tag Manager** snippet code was inserted in my case at the end of other meta tags, which caused the same problem.
The problem was solved after moving GTM snippet code to the **top** of meta tags.

author: lluzak

body:
The most likely reason is that by default, only files that are being used within the app are being traced (copied over). In your case, it sounds like you are "borrowing" the webpack compilation for that worker file. If it's not imported anywhere in the app, it might be left out from being copied over to the standalone folder.

You could add a small `cp` command to copy it over after build.

If you import it somewhere in the app, I think it should already be working without the extra webpack config.

If the latter is true, I would like to ask you to add a small reproduction repository with the files and the code so we can verify. Otherwise, let me know if the above suggestions resolve your issue. :+1:

body:
This is likely because you are either not using the import or using it in the page component. 

Next.js will try to eliminate imports from the client bundle that is used in data fetching methods like `getStaticProps` etc.

Otherwise, it will fail the build as `fs` is not an API in the browser. Make sure you use the import, and that it's only used in the data fetching methods.

You can test this on this page: https://next-code-elimination.vercel.app/

This is described on each data fetching method, example: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-static-props

Actually for us its: csurf > csrf > rndm
See https://github.com/pillarjs/csrf/blob/master/package.json#L12
author: eelcobosklopper

body:
Having looked more into this, my point from https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35048#issuecomment-1059680496 still stands.

`assert` is polyfilled for browser environments as well, for historical reasons, but the conclusion is that we don't include the polyfills, unless they are actually used.

In your particular case, `csurf` - which is created for Express.js - should not end up in a client bundle, so make sure it is only used server-side. If that's already ensured, the issue  is going to be in the underlying package `rndm`: https://github.com/crypto-utils/rndm/blob/a1bc3f838093e3b65a3c886dd9a061e3b98bd49c/index.js#L2

Where `assert` is `require`'ed, even when it is unnecessary in Node.js environments. It should be removed. Webpack tries to polyfill when you `require("assert")`

Your options could be yarn resolutions, patch-package, polyfilling `assert` yourself, or finding an alternative to `csurf`.

In any case, it is likely that the %eval% code is not invoked, so you might be good anyway.

body:
So, it turns out this issue is actually being caused by a fairly subtle error thrown in `_app.js`. We are attempting to get/set a cookie in `getInitialProps` and getting the error `Error: Cannot set headers after they are sent to the client`. This error does not appear in development mode or during the build.

Do you have a suggestion for where we should be doing this instead? This getting/setting needs to happen on every page and the value is then made available to an app-wide React Context, which is why we were doing it in `_app.js` in the first place.

body:
Each `*.module.css` file that is imported from your app is a separate CSS compilation. And so is the `global.css`. You can't expect that declarations in the global.css affect the `*.module.css` file. Even if that seem to work sometimes, that is not working as intended (probably some global variables in the postcss-custom-media plugin).

What's more likely to work is if you `@import` as shared `media.module.css` file from each `*.module.css` file where you want to use custom media queries. And place your declarations in that shared file.
This way each CSS compilation knows about the custom media declarations from the shared file.

body: @balazsorban44 This is what I meant. Take a look at the example below. Given the example in the screenshot ![Screenshot from 2022-03-09 13-27-43](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12301022/157441934-cddc2596-511c-410e-a139-01eb178d19cd.png) produces this HTML structure ![Screenshot from 2022-03-09 13-30-30](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12301022/157442242-76350088-abed-48db-adb0-a4418d13f6ab.png) HTML from the DOM (from the screenshot) ```html ``` and as you can see, the `width` and `height` **attributes** for the HTML image element **are not present**, but they **should be present** in the `` element. My feature request was to add the `width` and `height` attributes on the `` element for the production version of the Next.js application that is using the `` component. The original source for proposing this feature comes from this article https://web.dev/optimize-cls/?utm_source=lighthouse&utm_medium=lr#images-without-dimensions. This will help developers in gaining a higher score on the "[PageSpeed Insights](https://pagespeed.web.dev/)" web tool because the browser will reduce layout shifts since it knows how much space is required for the remote image, thus providing a better UX and overall better score for page performance.

body:
Could you be a bit more elaborate on the reproduction? How are you appending params etc? I would like to see your client-side code. (best would be a minimal reproduction repository without anything that is unnecessary to reproduce the issue you are having)

I'm not entirely sure I understand the goal of your `redirects` either. :thinking: You should use redirects to map one path to another.

> Redirects allow you to redirect an incoming request path to a different destination path.

https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/redirects

What are you trying to achieve?
author: balazsorban44

body:
> I'm not entirely sure I understand the goal of your redirects either. thinking You should use redirects to map one path to another.

Am (ab)using the redirects to create a proxy for the api requests. The app itself is in a mono-repo and is the only next app there. All the api requests are configured in shard-lib modules. These requests are only meant to be mapped during development (it's in PoC stage for now).

> best would be a minimal reproduction repository without anything that is unnecessary to reproduce the issue you are having

I created a repo for this purpose and realized that the issue is not with next after all. This PoC is a port from a classic CRA project over to next. As such we still have some client-side routing to do with `react-router`/`react-router-dom`. On top of this we have to preserve state between page navigations (which I managed to get working with [next-redux-wrapper](https://github.com/kirill-konshin/next-redux-wrapper). So now I'm thinking that the issue probably comes the integration there somehow.

I can also reproduce when using Yalc, ESM exports only and 12.1.0. The Yalc symlink is treated as a local package instead of an external, and thus is built again with Next, with "import" transformed as "require", leading to issues with subdependencies of the ESM dependency.

There might be unrelated issue about how cjs+esm exports works as well and maybe a similar issue with Yarn link.

body:
Here is a minimal reproduction: https://github.com/VulcanJS/repro-bug-yalc-next-12-1

A package that uses ES modules and depends on another package that also uses ES modules (say react-konva) cannot be linked via Yalc (and maybe with yarn link as well though I have only tested Yalc), as it will be treated as a local package by Next instead of an external. Next is probably tricked by the symlink to a local package, and thinks it should build that package.

body:
Same issue with Yarn 3 workspaces, with Next 12.1.6, that also use a symlink.
To reproduce:
```sh
git clone https://github.com/VulcanJS/vulcan-npm/tree/feature/remix-starter
cd vulcan-npm
yarn
cd starters/next
yarn run build
```

I get:
```sh
Failed to compile.

../../packages/mongo/mongoParams.ts
Module parse failed: The keyword 'interface' is reserved (79:0)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
| 
| // TODO: should we use TModel or Vulcan document here??
> interface FilterFunctionOutput {
|   selector: FilterQuery<any>;
|   options: QueryOptions;

Import trace for requested module:
../../packages/mongo/client/index.ts
../../packages/react-hooks/dist/index.js
./src/pages/vn/admin/crud/VulcanUser.tsx


> Build failed because of webpack errors
```

So `@vulcanjs/mongo`, which is a package of my monorepository, handled via Yarn 3 workspaces, and this symlinked in `starters/next/node_modules` pointing to `../../packages/mongo`, is somehow understood as some local code.
Next tries to read the actual TS source instead of respecting the `package.json` and reading `dist/index`.

Everytime the dependency of the dependency is specifically affected, not necessarily the first-level dependency (here "@vulcanjs/react-hooks" is ok, but not "@vulcanjs/mongo" that it depends on ; both are ES modules with exports map).

body:
I narrowed the problem down to version >14.1.4 in our application. Every Next version higher than that would block the dev mode.

For me adding `"@babel/runtime": "^7.24.6"` to the resolutions in `package.json` helped fix it. When running `yarn install`, it removed duplicate/stale/old versions. After that I removed the "resolution" and everything still works fine.

```json
"resolutions": {
  "@babel/runtime": "^7.24.6"
},
```

body:
@balazsorban44 Just to provide some additional context / info...

We are creating a `next/image` wrapper component (to be published in our SDK npm package) which uses a 
custom loader. According to [docs](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#built-in-loaders)/[discussions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/19065), this approach should support build-time optimization (`next build` and even `next export`). However, as noted in the description the `config` values (in particular, [we are using `path`](https://github.com/Sitecore/jss/blob/feature/449842-nextjs-image/packages/sitecore-jss-nextjs/src/components/NextImage.tsx#L21)) passed into the custom loader are not pulling from the app's next.config.js. Instead, they are the [default values](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.0/packages/next/server/image-config.ts#L52).

This issue does _not_ occur with `next dev`.
author: ambrauer

body:
Hi @ambrauer and @sc-addypathania 

The `config` parameter of the `loader()` function is [undocumented](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/image#loader) because its only meant to be used by the default loader.

It was changed from a global variable to a function parameter in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/33559 as an implementation detail, not as a public API.

 Furthermore, this config is meant for the default Image Optimization API. Since the `loader` prop bypasses the default Image Optimization API in favor of a custom function, those config options should no longer be relevant because the function can implement the optimization url however it wishes.

It sounds like the bug here is that `next dev` is accidentally leaking implementation details.

For the purposes of your custom loader, it might be best to ask the user for an explicit `prefix` prop rather than relying on next.config.js (especially if we ever decide to drop the `path` config option in a future release).
author: styfle

body:
Here's a concrete example. Consider this Next.js app:

```js
module.exports = {
  images: {
    loader: 'cloudinary',
    path: 'https://res.cloudinary.com/myaccount/'
  }
}
```

```js
import Image from 'next/image'
import OtherImage from 'other-image'

<Image src="/me.png" width="100" height="100" />

<OtherImage src="/me.png" width="100" height="100" />
```

The `path` only makes sense because the loader is cloudinary and that prefix is for cloudinary. It emits something like:

```
https://res.cloudinary.com/myaccount/q_auto,c_limit,w_100,q_auto/me.jpg
```

For the other image imported from a package that wraps `next/image`, it would likely break if it tried to reuse this path. It might emit something that doesn't work with cloudinary, such as:

```
https://res.cloudinary.com/myaccount/me.jpg?mw=100
```
author: styfle

body:
Hi @styfle, thanks for clearing this up. 

We were struggling with the fact that the URL root/path/prefix is defined in the _app_, while the custom loader is in our _SDK_. Piggybacking on the default loaders config was a simple solution for us (and made some sense from a DevEx perspective since "path" served the same purpose for us), but your explanation makes sense.

One suggestion beyond aligning the config treatment between `next dev` and `next build`: it might also be helpful to exclude the `config` from the component `loader` function parameter type (`ImageLoaderProps`) to avoid confusion. Folks implementing a custom loader will see this and assume they can use it 😊

We will explore other options for providing the root (such as a `prefix` prop).

Thanks again!

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body:
The current docs were written by @ijjk in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/26320. (Before then, there was nothing documenting this behavior?!)

@ijjk could you expand a bit on what you meant by "ensure the state is updated using `useEffect`", maybe with a minimal example? I'm currently working around the issue using React keys, which feels more idiomatic to me -- are there problems with using keys that I haven't considered?

Also: are there ways we could avoid other devs going on multi-hour debugging sessions, only to eventually find this note _after_ debugging? E.g.

* Could we teach this behavior in [the tutorial around the stuff on client-side navigation](https://nextjs.org/learn/basics/navigate-between-pages/client-side)?
* Could we get Next.js in dev mode to log something like "Note: re-rendering same component; state will be preserved"?
author: jameshfisher

body: I also think the documentation should explain _why_ the current behavior is as it is -- what's the advantage of it? Can I do anything cool with the current behavior, that makes up for its surprisingness? Currently the note in the docs reads like a bug description.
author: jameshfisher

body:
This seems like a React confusion though, not Next.js specific.

> What problems are there with this approach?

When you navigate between pages, the page component receives the new props (`pageTitle` in your case). Passing an argument to `useState` is telling React to initialize the state with that value, but you never update it using `setTitle`. See: https://reactjs.org/docs/hooks-reference.html#usestate

Adding this would work:

```js
useEffect(() => {
  setTitle(pageTitle)
}, [pageTitle])
```

but it's unnecessary in your example's case. If you rely on the updated `pageTitle` value during render, just use the `pageTitle` value directly.

I believe our docs is clear enough around this, but let me know how would you change it based on the above. :+1:

Maybe https://nextjs.org/learn/basics/navigate-between-pages/client-side could indeed have a note around this.
author: balazsorban44

body:
Note that my example above is artificially minimal. My real application is much larger, with pages using a [Zustand react context](https://github.com/pmndrs/zustand#react-context), which is initialized using Next.js props. I was very surprised to find that the Zustand context was not re-initialized when navigating to a new page. In the example above, I replaced all this with just a `useState` so as to not carry over the Zustand baggage into this issue.

I'm not sure that using `useEffect` to reset state is a good general solution. For example, I'm not sure how/where I'd do that with this Zustand context. It also feels easy to forget to add a `useEffect` for every piece of state. Using a React key feels cleaner, since React keys are specifically made to define distinct identities that should not share state. (I couldn't find this in official React docs, but [here's what I mean](https://kentcdodds.com/blog/understanding-reacts-key-prop#:~:text=This%20allows%20you%20to%20return%20the%20exact%20same%20element%20type%2C%20but%20force%20React%20to%20unmount%20the%20previous%20instance%2C%20and%20mount%20a%20new%20one.).)

Hey. Just an add-on. I was just going through the issues.

I agree with @balazsorban44 
React hooks are specific to the fact that you re-render only when any changes happen. And you change the data only using the hooks methods. If the react was made according to your assumptions, think about all the unnecessary changes that would occur even if they are not required. So you change the data, only which is needed to change (using useEffect). Otherwise it will just re-render with the previous data.

That is the whole point of hooks i guess. I might be wrong in understanding your point.

body:
IMHO it is not a bug of Next.js. Because not only `data-nscript` but any extra attributes (`type="application/javascript"`, or `type="text/javascript"`, or `language="javascript"`, or `charset="UTF-8"`) will throw the error as well:

```html
<!-- All of examples below will cause the error -->
<script type="application/javascript" src="..."></script>
<script type="text/javascript" src="..."></script>
<script language="javascript" src="..."></script>
<script charset="UTF-8" src="..."></script>
```

You should report the bug to the vendor instead.

body:
Is the data present on the html of the page (View Page Source)? If not, then opengraph tags won't work.
You most likely call that tag on a page component, which is wrapped in several few others in the `_app`. If any of them use a conditional render relying on the client-side logic, then they will always evaluate to `false` on the server and thus the entire page renders client-side only.

author: laurencefass

body:
>By render correctly in browser: I was initially checking the page as seen by chrome developer tools which (somehow) shows all of the correct headers even though they do not appear in the page source.

Dev tools shows you the state of DOM which is a parsed html with all eligible scripts in it activated. If you want to test things like opengraph tags and server-side render in general you have to turn off client javascript in your browser settings. AFAIK there is no way to do this on per-page/site basis without extensions.

>If thats true does this imply that Next JS is not rendering page metadata on the server?

No, but you have to be very careful how you write the code for server-rendered pages. Specifically a lot of react hooks do not work server-side, outside of initial state set by `React.useState()`. Chances are `useRouter()` is a client-side hook and, due it being a part of nextjs, it is treated "smartly" by the framework. Try to get `pathname` from `getServerSideProps`, pass it as props to the page component and see if it fixes the issue.

author: adamjarling

body: @adamjarling , you can use `next-seo` on the `_app.js` and then use `getStaticProps` on other pages and pass the required props. That way, when it renders, it'll go through `_app.js` so you're meta will get picked from there. If you place the meta in individual pages and have a seperate meta set in `_app.js` your app will override it.

body:
Current next version: `"next": "^11.1.3"`

The problem for me was like @GabenGar mentioned: I had some conditional logic in my component.

My component with dynamic route is in `pages/article/[slug].tsx`, where I used to fetch the article using the slug on component mount. I used to use `useRouter` to grab the slug in the URL → pass the slug to another `react-query` hook to fetch the article (`useFetchArticle`). This hook returns states like `isLoading`, so I used to do 

```
if(isLoading) {return <LoadingComponent/>}
```
This resulted in all of my overriding open graph tags to not show up. I changed the logic to fetching the article in `getServerSideProps` and removed all conditions in the component and everything worked.


I also used the `next/head` component to override the open graph tags defined in `_app.tsx`

```
<Head>
        <title>{title}</title>
        <meta property="og:image" content={hero_image} key="image" />
        <meta property="og:title" content={title} key="title" />
        <meta property="og:description" content={preview} key="description" />
      </Head>
```

body:
Even with the workarounds posted in this thread, I cannot get media previews working for any of my NexTJS websites using next-seo, or even hand-entered per page meta. Only the meta in my `_app` and `_document` show when viewing soure. This makes it nearly impossible to have a NextJS website with SEO.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1910461/230956380-f9887dda-073a-4818-8a0d-fc46c860bcbb.png)

The tags appear to be populated by javascript after load, making them nearly useless for search engines.

I cannot delete all the meta in `_app` (for one of these workarounds), since NextJS __requires__ your viewport be set there, and absolutely refuses to allow you to set it in `_document` (or pages for that matter) **

```
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
Warning: viewport meta tags should not be used in _document.js's <Head>. https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/no-docume
nt-viewport-meta
```

---

The `getServerSideProps` route would force **all pages to be dynamically rendered, significantly reducing performance and completely disabling the ability to statically render the site as a PWA.**

How can this possibly be an acceptable outcome?

To quote the build log, when building **without** standalone:

```
Route (pages)                                             Size     First Load JS
┌ λ /                                                     1.74 kB         245 kB
├   /_app                                                 0 B             243 kB
├ λ /pricing                                              3.18 kB         249 kB
├ λ /profile/[id]                                         15.4 kB         275 kB
├ λ /ref                                                  253 B           292 kB
├ λ /ref/[id]                                             213 B           292 kB
├ λ /server-sitemap.xml                                   257 B           243 kB
└ λ /test                                                 2.19 kB         245 kB

... [all pages were forced server-side rendered]

λ  (Server)  server-side renders at runtime (uses getInitialProps or getServerSideProps)
```

When building **with** standalone/export:

```
Error occurred prerendering page "/". Read more: https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/prerender-error
Error: Error for page /_app: pages with `getServerSideProps` can not be exported. See more info here:
 https://nextjs.org/docs/messages/gssp-export
    at .\node_modules\next\dist\export\worker.js:333:23
    at async Span.traceAsyncFn (.\node_modules\next\dist\trace\trace.js:79:20)
```

[To quote the documentation
](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/get-server-side-props)

---

Further, `_document` requires a `<Head>` even if it's empty:

```
Your custom Document (pages/_document) did not render all the required subcomponent.
Missing component: <Head />
```


---

Is static export deprecated, or is this a bug in need of fixing to restore a supported feature?

body:
> Possible Solution. > > I have spent days looking at this now and I am not convinced that the mechanism for pages overriding _app.tsx metadata by inclusion of a in individual pages works for facebook sharing . While the overrides show in the browser debugger they are not picked up by facebook debugger. > > To workaround this limitation and solve this particular issue I have been able to expose metadata to facebook debugger by having a single block in _app.tyx and pass page specific metata as props in getServerSideProps. Guessing would also work for getStaticProps. > > page.tsx > > ``` > > export async function getServerSideProps(context) { > return { > props: { > title: "Page one! ", > description: "Page One metadata", > image: `${hostURL}/images/page_one_image.jpg` > } > } > } > > export default function Page() { > return
>

Page One

>
> } > ``` > > _app.tsx > > ``` > function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }: AppProps) { > let title = pageProps.title ? pageProps.title : "Default metatags "; > let description = pageProps.description ? pageProps.description : "Default description"; > let image = pageProps.image ? pageProps.image : `${hostURL}/image/default_image.jpg`; > > return <> > > > > > > ``` thank you for this soln... this is actually quite elegant as I no longer need to have 'Head' elements on each page - I just use staticProps to pass whatever I need to pre-render. Works amazing with all sorts of social sharing and optimises SEO. GOLD

body: @agustints it looks like you are revalidating and then doing a client-transition. body:
@agustints is the revalidation API being called before calling `router.push`? Seems like `unstable_revalidate()` should be called inside of the edit endpoint after the data is saved instead of doing an additional request client-side. Then after the edit response is returned to the client it would need to clear the cache ``delete next.router.sdc[new URL(`/_next/data/${window.__NEXT_DATA__.buildId}/uy/adopciones/rocky.json`, location.href).toString()]` and then trigger the `router.push()``. 

Also it looks like a service-worker is present, so would be good to double check it isn't accidentally caching any `_next/data` requests.

body:
Hi, this has been updated in the latest canary of Next.js `v12.1.7-canary.48`, please update and give it a try!

In this version the API has been made stable so `unstable_revalidate` became `revalidate`, there is also optimistic updating during client-navigations where fresh data is attempted to be fetched in case an on-demand revalidation has occurred since the data was prefetched. 

A manual client cache update can also be triggered in the latest canary before a navigation with `next.router.prefetch('/blog/post-1', undefined, { unstable_skipClientCache: true })`

Please remove the duplicate revalidations for both the page and the data like mentioned in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/35195#issuecomment-1163374351 as this should not be necessary. If you are seeing issues with the data route not being revalidated please open a fresh issue with a Vercel deployment and we can investigate that closer! 

I'm gonna close this as it seems to have diverged from the initial issue which should be resolved after updating canary.

body: It's an optimization enabled by cssnano, See `mergeLonghand` https://cssnano.co/docs/what-are-optimisations/.
author: wwwdepot

body:
Next.js is using a custom variant of the `cssnano-preset-default` preset, source here: https://github.com/Timer/cssnano-preset-simple

Adding `mergeLonghand: false` ~to that and re-compile it for Next.js~ here:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/f14f62838aa080d40bbff124f17d698e55b99e62/packages/next/build/webpack/plugins/css-minimizer-plugin.ts#L47

might fix the issue, but it looks like `cssnano` acknowledges this as a bug and we should probably wait for a proper fix on their side: https://github.com/cssnano/cssnano/issues/675 :thinking: (admittedly though, that issue has been opened for 3 years now).


Another downside would be that it could affect most current CSS outputs negatively, so the workaround has a cost.

author: wasd171

body:
So I think the issue is that `cssnano-simple@3.0.0` depends on `cssnano-preset-simple@^3.0.0`. So the package manager resolves `next@12.0.0` -> `cssnano-simple@3.0.0` -> `cssnano-preset-simple@3.0.1`. 
I've pinned my `cssnano-preset-simple` to `3.0.0` and build works again.

Seems like it should be fixed in master already https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/35258

body:
Please verify that your issue can be recreated with `next@canary`.

### **Why was this issue marked with the `please verify canary` label?**

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

### **Useful Resources**

- [How to Contribute to Open Source (Next.js)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cuoNzXFLitc)
- [How to create a Minimal, Complete, and Verifiable example](https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve)
- [Reporting a Next.js bug](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/1.bug_report.yml)
- [Next.js Triaging issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing/repository/triaging.md)

body: Could you tell us about the use case of serving 10M files? Are you referencing these files from your app?
author: balazsorban44

body:
@balazsorban44 sorry for the delay, I somehow missed the notification.
We're serving that amount of static assets (documents mainly) and yes, they're referenced by the app. To be clear, I just wanted to confirm whether this is the expected behavior and we shouldn't use Next.js for this use case (and probably state it more clearly in the documentation) or maybe it's something to improve in the framework. Thanks in advance!
author: santigimeno

body:
You can use Next.js just fine for this use case, just not for serving 10 million files from `public`. `public` is considered an immutable directory which is read during bootup to ensure that only the files that exist during bootup can be served for the duration of the process. This was done to ensure there is no way out of the directory itself for serving given that any other path would not be served. It was added after a few directory traversal vectors were uncovered over the years.

I think the note on using external file hosts should be expanded to reflect that it's recommended to keep under a certain amount of files in `public`.

body:
Given the above steps, I am unable to reproduce this (in neither dev nor production) :thinking:. `TransformStream` is polyfilled when not available:

https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/694407450638b037673c6d714bfe4126aeded740/packages/next/server/node-polyfill-web-streams.js#L7

Could you create a small reproduction repository that I can use to investigate further? :pray:
author: balazsorban44

body:
> Given the above steps, I am unable to reproduce this (in neither dev nor production) 🤔. `TransformStream` is polyfilled when not available:
> 
> https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/694407450638b037673c6d714bfe4126aeded740/packages/next/server/node-polyfill-web-streams.js#L7
> 
> Could you create a small reproduction repository that I can use to investigate further? 🙏


hello,

In that file, adding a check for TransformStream fixes the issue for me.

```javascript
if (!global.ReadableStream || !global.TransformStream) {
    global.ReadableStream = _readableStream.ReadableStream;
    global.TransformStream = _webStreamsPolyfill.TransformStream;
}
```
Also, I've tested with other projects and this issue doesn't occur.


edit: I've determined the problem to come from using the 'mailgun.js' library. The error may be reproduced by adding mailgun.js to package.json and requiring from server.js. The issue only occurs on v12.0.11-canary.7+. I've updated the original post.
author: CRAKZOR
CRAKZOR

body: Polyfills aren't applied to the `server.js` module, so the issue is that your dependency probably relies on `TransformStream` but does not polyfill it. You might want to report it in their repo if you can verify the issue is certainly with Mailgun

body:
We had the exact same issue, but we noticed that router.asPath() was behaving differently on my local (and browser) and on the server, instead of returning the expected '/' it returns '/index' and here is where it got appended to the canonical URL in our case. So we dug deeper and found that this issue was only happening on Node 18x, we downgraded the Vercel function to 16x and it disappeared.

A workaround that we found to make it work with Node 18x was to check the path before using it:

`const path = router.asPath === '/index' ? '/' : router.asPath;`
author: juancarloselorriaga

body:
We've had the same issue on our codebase, `/index` was appearing after first load. We tried to override canonical url by using `next-seo` package inside our homepage file like this by adding homepage domain url from our env variable: 

`<NextSeo canonical={process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_DOMAIN} />`

And that worked for every page load!

body: @balazsorban44 The linked article cites middleware runtime constraints, but nothing about how dependencies are built. As a user this overloading of the browser field is surprising -- I would not expect to have the browser field applied to a build that is not executed on the client's browser. User's may want to consume a different library entry point or ensure it doesn't end up in client builds irrespective of any runtime constraints.

We noticed the provided reproduction was using an older version of Next.js, instead of `canary`.

The canary version of Next.js ships daily and includes all features and fixes that have not been released to the stable version yet. You can think of canary as a _public beta_. Some issues may already be fixed in the canary version, so please verify that your issue reproduces by running `npm install next@canary` and test it in your project, using your reproduction steps.

If the issue does not reproduce with the `canary` version, then it has already been fixed and this issue can be closed.

### **How can I quickly verify if my issue has been fixed in `canary`?**

The safest way is to install `next@canary` in your project and test it, but you can also search through [closed Next.js issues](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aclosed) for duplicates or check the [Next.js releases](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases). You can also use the GitHub [template](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) (preferred), or the [CodeSandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) or [StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com/fork/github/vercel/next.js/tree/canary/examples/reproduction-template) templates to create a reproduction with `canary` from scratch.

### **My issue has been open for a long time, why do I need to verify `canary` now?**

Next.js does not backport bug fixes to older versions of Next.js. Instead, we are trying to introduce only a minimal amount of breaking changes between major releases.

### **What happens if I don't verify against the canary version of Next.js?**

An issue with the `please verify canary` that receives no meaningful activity (e.g. new comments that acknowledge verification against `canary`) will be automatically closed and locked after 30 days.

If your issue has not been resolved in that time and it has been closed/locked, please open a new issue, with the required reproduction, using `next@canary`.

### **I did not open this issue, but it is relevant to me, what can I do to help?**

Anyone experiencing the same issue is welcome to provide a minimal reproduction following the above steps. Furthermore, you can upvote the issue using the :+1: reaction on the topmost comment (please **do not** comment "I have the same issue" without repro steps). Then, we can sort issues by votes to prioritize.

### **I think my reproduction is good enough, why aren't you looking into it quicker?**

We look into every Next.js issue and constantly monitor open issues for new comments.

However, sometimes we might miss one or two due to the popularity/high traffic of the repository. We apologize, and kindly ask you to refrain from tagging core maintainers, as that will usually not result in increased priority.

Upvoting issues to show your interest will help us prioritize and address them as quickly as possible. That said, every issue is important to us, and if an issue gets closed by accident, we encourage you to open a new one linking to the old issue and we will look into it.

body: Oh, we actually document that `\\:` should work. https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next.config.js/rewrites#regex-path-matching
author: jankaifer

body:
> I revisited this some time ago and I've found a workaround using Next13:
> 
> ```
> const nextConfig = {
>   reactStrictMode: true,
>   async rewrites() {
>     return [
>       {
>         source: "/\\+\\+api\\+\\+/:slug*",
>         destination: "http://localhost:8080/Plone/%2B%2Bapi%2B%2B/:slug*",
>       },
>     ];
>   },
> };
> ```
> 
> using the escaped characters seems to work correctly.

This workaround does not work on colons in the destination URL when the destination URL is a REST API. i.e. using `%3A` instread of `:` produces a 404 not found on APIs that I have tested with.

author: jesstelford

body:
Let me share my research.

Before `v12.1.6-canary.13`, js files were transformed, if using swc, in two ways (details are omitted):
- through [`jest-transformer.js`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next/build/swc/jest-transformer.js)
  - [`swc/index.js::transformSync()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next/build/swc/index.js#L217)
  - [`transform.rs::transform_sync()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/napi/src/transform.rs#L237)
  - [`transform.rs::exec_transform()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/napi/src/transform.rs#L191)

- through [`next-swc-loader.js`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next/build/webpack/loaders/next-swc-loader.js)
  - [`swc/index.js::transform()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next/build/swc/index.js#L212)
  - [`transform.rs::transform()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/napi/src/transform.rs#L228)
  - [`transform.rs::schedule_transform()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/napi/src/transform.rs#L159)
  - [`lib.rs::custom_before_pass()`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/core/src/lib.rs#L109)
  - [`relay.rs`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.12/packages/next-swc/crates/core/src/relay.rs) (`` `graphql` `` TaggedTemplates is checked here)

I can't read Rust code, so don't know what is the difference between `transform()` and `transform_sync()`.
But the problem was there: `jest-transformer.js` didn't invoke `relay.rs`.

Now things have changed.
Since [`v12.1.6-canary.13`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v12.1.6-canary.13/packages/next-swc/crates/napi/src/transform.rs), the `exec_transform()` has been removed and the two transformations are both using `schedule_transform()`, which means `jest-transformer.js` may work as expected.

However there is another problem: `relay.rs` requires `pages_dir` parameter (see #33918).

`next-swc-loader.js` sets `pagesDir` using [`get-project-dir.ts`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/af1d7c94f62ea403777adc3e480cc212c5bf6290/packages/next/lib/get-project-dir.ts), so it is okay.

But `jest-transformer.js` not sets `pagesDir` and it even cannot simply use `get-project-dir.ts`:
- `get-project-dir.ts` has been depending on [`next.ts`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/af1d7c94f62ea403777adc3e480cc212c5bf6290/packages/next/bin/next.ts) since #34836, so just importing `get-project-dir.ts` starts dev([default command](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/af1d7c94f62ea403777adc3e480cc212c5bf6290/packages/next/bin/next.ts#L16)) server, which is an unexpected side effect.
- `getProjectDir()` needs `dir` parameter passed in [`<dir>`](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/af1d7c94f62ea403777adc3e480cc212c5bf6290/packages/next/cli/next-build.ts#L39) argument in cli commands. If passing in `<dir>` explicitly in cli, there's no way to tell `jest-transformer.js` what `<dir>` is.

It is not smooth, but adding `pagesDir` parameter lets `` `graphql` `` be processed correctly with Jest anyway.
And it's just an experiment like POC, so don't try this at work :)

Hope this helps you understand what's going on behind the scenes.
author: wonu

body: nice investigative work @wonu!
author: damassi

body: @wonu thanks for having a look at this 👏 I've opened a PR that adds `pagesDir` to the transformer: #36599

body: Seems to be working well with TypeScript here. This is a great addition, it solves many issues with layout that I've experienced while using Image. But still doesn't cover the one use case I think the Image component lacks the most. > What layout / styling behavior were you previously unable to accomplish with next/image? The image component desperately needs a way to define art direction. That is, to be able to provide different images at different breakpoints. i.e, something that matches the following: ``` ``` > What layout modes did you try? > Did the experimental ”raw” layout mode solve your problem?  - If not, what changes would allow it to solve the problem? I've tried all layout modes. The problem stems from the fact that the developer has very limited control over the generated srcsets. Either you use `responsive` or `fill` and have your image generated in like 10 different sizes (none of which are relevant because I only want my image generated in 1x or 2x size). Or you define width & height while using `fixed` or `raw` and get precise 1x and 2x sizes. But there is no way to do that while also defining a different image for a different viewport. I think that it should be possible to allow for the user to supply alternative `src` (with separate `width` and `height` for each) for alternative media breakpoints. > Do you have any additional feedback about the new mode or its documentation? The documentation lacks a demo, instead there is a link to "Demo Background Image" which is a bit confusing since it doesn't seem to be using `raw`.
author: OskarGroth
pdkn

body:
I investigated (https://github.com/swc-project/swc/pull/4273), and I think the cause is

 - identifiers are reused by terser, with the `e` being the most common one.
 - AST optimizer does not care about variable scoping, thanks to the special trick swc uses
   - (This is common to almost all codes of swc. We use a very different approach for scoping)
 - the trick requires name mangler, but it’s disabled because eval is used
 - the top-level `e` is overridden by one of other `e`s


So making the AST optimizer aware of the scope should fix it.

author: wdtamagi

body:
At first I thought I was doing something wrong. But I'm experiencing the same issue as you. I can see that the revalidate call is actually being made. I can see multiple requests coming in (I revalidate multiple endpoints) with the header `x-prerender-revalidate`.
I can see that my app endpoints (non-dynamic-routes) are rerendering and fetching data. All of the simple routes are revalidated correctly. But this is not the case for dynamic routes e.g. `/nieuws/[section]/[id]/[title].tsx`.
It seems to only revalidate correctly when the `revalidate` timer has passed. I've found this when switching to revalidate `3600` and have seen that nothing happens. After switching back to `30` I can see the revalidations happen more frequently.

So my guess is that this is a bug which happens when using on-demand-isr in combination with dynamic routes.

Edit:
Another thing to note is that I was using i18n config in next.js. After removing this config it seems that on-demand isr works normally again.

author: bomzj

body:
I still don't understand why lowercase or uppercase driveletters are a problem in the first place. All officially available filesystems for Windows are case-insensitive. And therefore paths on a Windows filesystem should always be considered case-insensitive. Also, it's the drive letter, while the app lives at a (much) deeper level in the directory structure. Why is it ever even _considering_ parent directory names?

And sure, you could be working off a networked drive that is case-sensitive. But that's far less likely, so it makes more sense to remove the case-sensitiveness, than to risk problems with a hypothetical case-sensitive filesystem on Windows. And _even then_ it's totally fine if paths only differ by casing.
author: thany

body:
I found that running `cmd` from Windows 11 start menu always UPPERCASE drive letter which is fine in this case, so no issues with Next.js. But running `cmd` command in Total Commander results in lowercase drive letter. 
To fix Total Commander lowercasing, I added `DrivesShowUpcase=1` config parameter to `C:\Users\[username]\AppData\Roaming\GHISLER\wincmd.ini` and no more complaints from Next.js.
author: bomzj

body:
Sure, but the solution is not to somehow get Windows to always uppercase the driveletter. Because even if the driveletter has always been lowercase, Next.js still makes a problem out of it. The solution, therefore, is to make sure it doesn't matter.

Windows is case-insensitive, and should be treated as such. Same reason why Linux is treated as case-sensitive, because it is.

Anyway, I still want to know:

1. Why does it make a problem out of paths that only differ in casing on a case-insenstive filesystem?
2. Why does it consider driveletters to be a part of a module path? I.e. why are module paths not relative?

There _is_ something for this already! Yay! 

I'll share this here so anyone else looking for the answer can find it.

Add `passHref` to the `<Link>` component to force `<Link>` to pass its `href` to the child component. In my case, that works in React Bootstrap with things like `<NavBar.Brand>` and more.

More information: https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/next/link#if-the-child-is-a-custom-component-that-wraps-an-a-tag

body:
Looks like this is a firefox bug indeed. I found these:

https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/21459
https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=654072
https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/7292

The fix for now is to set `autoComplete='off`, unfortunately this is a firefox only property so it's not in button prop interface, but you can trick typescript by spreading the autoComplete like this:

```ts
const extraProps = {
  autoComplete: 'off'
}
return <button {...extraProps}></button>
```

body:
In the most recent canary we revamped how Middleware plays with configuration rewrites. As of this new version we will execute middleware **before** any configuration rewrites which means that if you have a configuration rewrite that points from `A` to `B` and there is a middleware, it will see the request going to `A` but not the final destination `B`.

To overcome this use case you can simply move the rewrite from configuration to the middleware and then execute the behaviour you want in there. I'm going to close the issue as the way it works currently in canary is how we expected it to work but of course feedback is more than welcome.

Feel free to open a new issue to drop feedback if you feel it doesn't make sense 🙏 
Thanks for reporting :)
